api: Linux epoll (epoll_create1 / epoll_ctl / epoll_wait), as consumed by runloom's netpoll backend (src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc, netpoll_pump.c.inc, netpoll_wake_iouring.c.inc, mn_sched_hub_main.c.inc). Rules audited against man epoll(7)/epoll_ctl(2), the Go runtime epoll netpoller, libuv, and CPython selectors, and cross-checked with runnable probes against the live 6.17 kernel.

rules.name: et-register-once-drops-partially-consumed-readiness
rules.rule: With EPOLLET the kernel reports a member fd only on an edge (a NOT-ready -> ready transition). If a fd is ADDed once and never EPOLL_CTL_MOD'd, epoll_wait delivers each edge exactly once; a waiter that links AFTER the edge was already reaped, or that consumes only part of the ready data and re-parks without draining to EAGAIN, gets NO further report until the next fresh edge. Correct ET use therefore REQUIRES drain-to-EAGAIN on every wake; a 'register-per-direction-once' arm cache that skips the re-ADD is only sound under LEVEL, never under ET.
rules.source: man epoll(7) 'Level-triggered and edge-triggered' + EPOLLET flag description ('edge-triggered ... an event is only delivered when a change occurs'); Go src/runtime/netpoll_epoll.go epollctl ADD uses _EPOLLET and REQUIRES the runtime drain the fd to EAGAIN (netpollBlock re-arms via pollDesc, not a skipped epoll_ctl); runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:9-20,266-281 (LEVEL, NO EPOLLET, register-per-direction-once) and the header warning lines 19-21 'Do NOT restore an EPOLLET register-once arm ... it reintroduced exactly the edge-drop hangs'.
rules.footgun: Assuming 'ADD once + never re-arm' behaves like level (re-reports while data remains). Under ET a parker that partially consumes and re-parks, or links late relative to the edge, is silently edge-dropped and hangs forever with data sitting readable in the socket buffer. This is the direct cause of runloom Hang A.
rules.probe: C: socketpair(sv); ep=epoll_create1(0); ADD sv[0] with EPOLLIN|EPOLLET; write(sv[1],"ab",2); epoll_wait(ep,,100) -> expect n==1 (edge). read(sv[0],&c,1) consuming only 1 of 2 buffered bytes; epoll_wait(ep,,100) again -> ET returns n==0 (VERIFIED on 6.17) although 1 byte is still buffered. PREDICTED-IF-MISREAD-AS-LEVEL: n==1. A register-once cache that treated this as 'still armed, skip' would hang. Diff n_observed(0) vs n_level_predicted(1); fail loudly if they match.
rules.bugs_addressed: 18/20 (EPOLLET|EXCLUSIVE register-once edge drop, Hang A, 42%->1%); the netpoll arm-cache skip path (netpoll_register.c.inc register-per-direction-once) is ONLY correct because the backend is LEVEL, not ET.

rules.name: level-rereports-every-wait-so-register-once-is-safe
rules.rule: A LEVEL-triggered member (default, no EPOLLET) is reported by EVERY epoll_wait for as long as the ready condition holds. This makes 'ADD once per direction and skip epoll_ctl on re-park' correct: a late-linking parker, a partial consumer, or a lost-then-relinked waiter is re-delivered on the next pump cycle with zero syscalls and no pending-bitmap. The cost is that an always-ready direction (a connected socket's EPOLLOUT) will busy-report on every wait unless its arm is dropped when no writer is parked.
rules.source: man epoll(7) 'level-triggered ... epoll_wait will return as long as the condition holds'; libuv src/unix/epoll.c uses level-triggered epoll and re-checks w->pevents each loop; runloom netpoll_register.c.inc:266-281 (LEVEL register-per-direction-once, 'IN never busy-loops ... OUT armed only when a WRITE waiter exists') and netpoll_pump.c.inc:58-67 (drop the OUT arm when no writer is parked to stop level busy-spin); spin model tools/verify/spin/netpoll_rearm.pml lines 24-40 'WHAT ACTUALLY CLOSES IT (the LEVEL register-once contract)'.
rules.footgun: Two opposite traps: (1) porting a LEVEL arm-cache to ET (drops edges, above); (2) arming EPOLLOUT LEVEL and leaving it armed after the writer wakes -> the pump spins at 100% CPU because a connected socket is perpetually writable. OUT must be disarmed when no WRITE waiter remains; IN is self-limiting (a not-readable socket produces no event).
rules.probe: C: socketpair(sv); ep; ADD sv[0] EPOLLIN (LEVEL, no ET); write(sv[1],"ab",2); loop 3x { epoll_wait(ep,,50); read 1 byte } -> LEVEL returns n==1 on iterations 1 and 2 (data still buffered), n==0 on iteration 3 (drained). VERIFIED: level re-reports the still-buffered byte. Contrast with the ET probe which returns n==0 on iteration 2. Separately: ADD EPOLLOUT LEVEL on a connected socket, call epoll_wait 5x without writing -> observe n==1 EVERY time (busy-report) to justify the OUT-disarm.
rules.bugs_addressed: 18/20 fix invariant; netpoll arm cache correctness; the EPOLLOUT level-busy-loop guard at netpoll_pump.c.inc:58-67.

rules.name: epollexclusive-wakes-one-or-more-not-exactly-one
rules.rule: EPOLLEXCLUSIVE only guarantees that 'one or more' of the epoll fds attached to a shared target are woken -- NOT exactly one. It reduces the thundering herd for MULTIPLE waiters on ONE epoll instance, but across SEPARATE epoll instances the kernel may wake several. EPOLLEXCLUSIVE may be set ONLY via EPOLL_CTL_ADD (EPOLL_CTL_MOD with it -> EINVAL), cannot be combined with EPOLLONESHOT (-> EINVAL), and the allowed event bits are a restricted subset.
rules.source: man epoll_ctl(2) EPOLLEXCLUSIVE ('one or more of the epoll file descriptors will receive an event'; 'EPOLL_CTL_MOD ... EINVAL'; 'if EPOLLEXCLUSIVE has been set ... and a subsequent EPOLL_CTL_MOD ... EINVAL'; ONESHOT combo EINVAL); runloom netpoll_wake_iouring.c.inc:396-407,610-621 (ADD-only, EINVAL fallback to EPOLLIN|EPOLLET without EXCLUSIVE); netpoll_register.c.inc:281 'EPOLLEXCLUSIVE stays OFF' for socket fds.
rules.footgun: Relying on EPOLLEXCLUSIVE to deliver an edge to EXACTLY ONE hub and then combining it with EPOLLET register-once: the kernel may wake multiple hubs (or, with ET, deliver the single edge to one that loses the park-commit race and drops it). Also: trying to widen an EPOLLEXCLUSIVE arm with EPOLL_CTL_MOD, or re-arming an EPOLLEXCLUSIVE|EPOLLONESHOT fd, silently fails with EINVAL and the fd is never re-armed.
rules.probe: C: efd=eventfd(0,NONBLOCK); e1=epoll_create1(0), e2=epoll_create1(0); ADD efd to BOTH with EPOLLIN|EPOLLEXCLUSIVE; write(efd,1); epoll_wait(e1,,50) and epoll_wait(e2,,50). VERIFIED on 6.17: BOTH return n==1 -- EPOLLEXCLUSIVE across separate epoll fds woke both, refuting 'exactly one'. Then epoll_ctl(e1,EPOLL_CTL_MOD,efd,{EPOLLIN|EPOLLEXCLUSIVE}) -> VERIFIED rc==-1 errno==EINVAL; epoll_ctl(e3,ADD,efd,{EPOLLIN|EPOLLEXCLUSIVE|EPOLLONESHOT}) -> VERIFIED rc==-1 errno==EINVAL. Fail if MOD/combo ever returns 0.
rules.bugs_addressed: 18/20 (EPOLLET|EXCLUSIVE edge drop -- the EXCLUSIVE half: 'one or more' + ET single edge = a woken-but-losing hub drops it); the io_uring/wake-eventfd EPOLLEXCLUSIVE arms at netpoll_wake_iouring.c.inc which MUST use ADD-only and the EINVAL fallback.

rules.name: epolloneshot-disables-until-mod-rearm-not-del-add
rules.rule: After an EPOLLONESHOT event is pulled out by epoll_wait, the fd is internally DISABLED (no further events, of any kind, including EPOLLERR/EPOLLHUP) but REMAINS registered. To re-arm you MUST call EPOLL_CTL_MOD with a fresh event mask; EPOLL_CTL_ADD returns EEXIST because the fd is still in the set. This costs one epoll_ctl per event -- the churn LEVEL register-once was adopted to eliminate.
rules.source: man epoll(7) EPOLLONESHOT ('after an event ... is pulled out ... the associated fd is internally disabled ... you MUST call epoll_ctl with EPOLL_CTL_MOD to rearm'); man epoll_ctl(2) EEXIST (ADD on an already-registered fd); runloom netpoll_register.c.inc:273-276 ('the win over the old LEVEL+EPOLLONESHOT scheme's one epoll_ctl/park', 'EPOLLONESHOT is gone').
rules.footgun: (1) Re-arming a ONESHOT fd with EPOLL_CTL_ADD -> EEXIST, arm silently fails, fd stays disabled, waiter hangs. (2) Assuming a disabled-by-oneshot fd will still deliver EPOLLERR/HUP -- it will NOT until re-MOD'd, so a peer close between the oneshot fire and the re-arm is missed. (3) A window between the oneshot event and the MOD re-arm where a newly-arriving edge is coalesced (harmless under level-semantics-in-MOD, but a lost wake if you never re-arm).
rules.probe: C: efd=eventfd(0,NONBLOCK); ep; ADD efd EPOLLIN|EPOLLONESHOT; write(efd,1); epoll_wait(ep,,100)->n==1. WITHOUT reading efd (counter still nonzero => still readable), epoll_wait(ep,,100) again -> observe n==0 (disabled by oneshot despite readable). epoll_ctl(ep,EPOLL_CTL_ADD,efd,...) -> observe EEXIST (still registered). epoll_ctl(ep,EPOLL_CTL_MOD,efd,{EPOLLIN|EPOLLONESHOT}) -> 0; epoll_wait(ep,,100) -> n==1 (re-armed delivers the still-readable counter). Diff: predicted n==0 on the second pre-rearm wait and EEXIST on the ADD; fail if either differs.
rules.bugs_addressed: Justifies why runloom dropped EPOLLONESHOT for LEVEL register-once (netpoll_register.c.inc:273-281); the netpoll arm cache's EEXIST->MOD and ENOENT->ADD fallbacks (register.c.inc:413,423) exist precisely because ADD/MOD are not interchangeable on an already/never-registered fd.

rules.name: err-hup-always-delivered-mask0-rdhup-must-be-requested
rules.rule: EPOLLERR and EPOLLHUP are ALWAYS reported by epoll_wait if they occur -- you do not (and cannot) request them, and they can arrive with NEITHER EPOLLIN nor EPOLLOUT set (a 'mask==0 for the requested directions' event). EPOLLRDHUP, by contrast, MUST be explicitly included in the registered event mask to be delivered. A dispatcher that maps epoll events to waiters by testing only EPOLLIN/EPOLLOUT will drop the wake of a fd that becomes only err/hung-up; err/hup must be folded into BOTH read and write directions.
rules.source: man epoll_ctl(2): EPOLLERR 'epoll_wait(2) will always report for this event; it is not necessary to set it in events'; EPOLLHUP 'epoll_wait(2) will always wait for this event; it is not necessary to set it'; EPOLLRDHUP is listed as a settable request bit. Go src/runtime/netpoll.go netpollready folds: read-ready if events & (EPOLLIN|EPOLLRDHUP|EPOLLHUP|EPOLLERR), write-ready if events & (EPOLLOUT|EPOLLHUP|EPOLLERR); CPython Lib/selectors.py EpollSelector.select folds (event & ~EPOLLIN)->WRITE, (event & ~EPOLLOUT)->READ; runloom netpoll_pump.c.inc:44-51 folds EPOLLERR/HUP/RDHUP into BOTH RUNLOOM_NETPOLL_READ and _WRITE.
rules.footgun: Registering EPOLLOUT-only (or EPOLLIN-only) and then keying the dispatcher on that same bit: when the peer closes, the kernel delivers EPOLLHUP (and maybe EPOLLERR) possibly WITHOUT the requested direction bit, the dispatcher sees 'not my bit', and the parked reader/writer is never woken -> hang. Symmetrically, expecting EPOLLRDHUP without having requested it yields silence.
rules.probe: C: socketpair(cv); ep; ADD cv[0] with events=EPOLLOUT ONLY (no IN, no HUP, no RDHUP requested); close(cv[1]); epoll_wait(ep,out,,100). VERIFIED on 6.17: n==1, out.events==0x14 == EPOLLOUT|EPOLLHUP -- EPOLLHUP delivered although never requested. A dispatcher testing only 'events & EPOLLIN' to wake a reader would see 0 and drop it. Assert (out.events & EPOLLHUP)!=0 while it was never in the registered mask; fail if EPOLLHUP is absent. Second probe: ADD with events=EPOLLIN only (no RDHUP), shutdown(peer,SHUT_WR) -> observe EPOLLRDHUP is NOT set (must be requested).
rules.bugs_addressed: The netpoll_pump.c.inc:44-51 both-directions fold; prevents the mask==0/err-only lost-wake class that mirrors bug-18-family hangs but via the dispatch mapping rather than the arm.

rules.name: epoll-ctl-error-set-add-eexist-mod-del-enoent-perm-badf
rules.rule: The epoll_ctl error set that a fallback layer depends on: EPOLL_CTL_ADD of an already-registered fd -> EEXIST; EPOLL_CTL_MOD or EPOLL_CTL_DEL of a not-registered fd -> ENOENT; a fd whose underlying file does not support epoll (regular file, directory) -> EPERM on ADD; a closed/invalid epfd or fd -> EBADF; ENOSPC when max_user_watches is exceeded; EINVAL for epfd==fd, non-epoll epfd, or a bad EPOLLEXCLUSIVE combination; ELOOP for nesting > 5 deep or a cycle. Closing the last reference to a fd auto-removes it from every epoll set, so a later DEL of a reused number can hit ENOENT legitimately.
rules.source: man epoll_ctl(2) ERRORS section (EBADF, EEXIST, EINVAL, ELOOP, ENOENT, ENOMEM, ENOSPC, EPERM) and NOTES 'a fd is removed ... automatically when all fds referring to the underlying open file description have been closed'; runloom netpoll_register.c.inc:408-427 (ADD then EEXIST->MOD; MOD then ENOENT->ADD), :578-579 (MOD ENOENT->ADD), and validate_arm treating EBADF/EPERM as 'fd genuinely dead -> clear cache + error-wake' (netpoll.c:145-146 stale-arm probe; RUNTIME_GOTCHAS.md epoll section).
rules.footgun: Treating ADD and MOD as interchangeable, or not handling the auto-removal-on-close race: (1) a MOD to widen a direction races a close and returns ENOENT -> if not retried as ADD the widen is lost and the second-direction waiter hangs; (2) an ADD after a concurrent sibling ADD returns EEXIST -> if not retried as MOD it raises a spurious FileExistsError out of a healthy wait_fd; (3) treating EPERM/EBADF as transient and re-parking on a fd the kernel can never poll -> a permanent hang instead of a raised error.
rules.probe: C: ep=epoll_create1(0). ADD an eventfd twice -> VERIFIED second==-1 EEXIST. MOD a never-registered eventfd -> VERIFIED -1 ENOENT. DEL that same never-registered fd -> ENOENT. ADD open("/etc/hostname",O_RDONLY) -> VERIFIED -1 EPERM (regular file not pollable). ADD with a closed epfd -> EBADF. Each observed errno is diffed against the predicted constant; a mismatch (e.g. ADD-twice returning 0, or regular-file ADD succeeding) fails the probe and invalidates the EEXIST->MOD / ENOENT->ADD / EPERM-is-fatal fallbacks.
rules.bugs_addressed: The register EEXIST->MOD and ENOENT->ADD fallbacks (netpoll_register.c.inc:408-427); the stale-arm probe's EBADF/EPERM='fd dead, error-wake' branch (netpoll.c stale-arm probe, the fd-poison heal landed 2026-07-05); commit 3f34bdd4 'error-wake stranded parkers on migration DEL-ok + ADD-fail'.

rules.name: poll-on-epoll-fd-is-readable-but-nested-et-excl-readiness-can-be-missed-under-load
rules.rule: An epoll fd is itself pollable: it reports readable (POLLIN) to an outer poll/epoll_wait whenever it holds ready events, and epolls may be nested up to depth 5. In the naive single-shot case this propagates for LEVEL and even ET|EXCLUSIVE members. BUT the outer readable-notification is edge-like with respect to a concurrent inner drainer: an ET / EPOLLEXCLUSIVE member whose readiness is consumed by one hub's inner epoll_wait does not RELIABLY re-signal the outer poll on another hub. So a scheduler that waits on the epoll fd via an outer poll (the io_uring loop 'UD_EPOLL' bridge) can miss a member edge and must unconditionally drain the inner epoll on every idle tick rather than only when the outer poll fires F_EPOLL.
rules.source: man epoll(7) Q6/Q7 (an epoll fd can be monitored by another epoll/poll/select; max nesting 5); runloom mn_sched_hub_main.c.inc:901-931 ('The poll-on-the-epoll-fd bridge can MISS a socket-readable edge ... EPOLLEXCLUSIVE readiness does NOT reliably propagate to a poll-on-the-epoll-fd (the UD_EPOLL bridge) -- so F_EPOLL can miss it and a fiber parked in file_read would hang') and the mitigation: unconditional runloom_netpoll_pump(0) + runloom_iouring_drain() each idle iteration; RUNTIME_GOTCHAS.md io_uring-loop section 'unless the loop idle path drains the global ring directly after loop_wait'.
rules.footgun: Assuming the outer poll(epoll_fd) is a complete, edge-safe notification of inner readiness and gating the inner drain on 'did the outer poll report the epoll fd'. Under M:N + free-threaded 3.13t with ET/EXCLUSIVE members, the F_EPOLL notification can be missed, the inner ready fd is left undrained, its parked reader hangs, and the deadlock census is blind because I/O-parked fibers keep has_wakeable_work() true (the cov_workload --hubs 4 loop-backend hang).
rules.probe: C: inner=epoll_create1(0), outer=epoll_create1(0), efd=eventfd(NONBLOCK); ADD efd->inner with EPOLLIN|EPOLLET|EPOLLEXCLUSIVE; ADD inner->outer with EPOLLIN. write(efd,1); then (IMPORTANT: sequence the call before reading revents -- C arg eval order is unspecified) int n=poll(&{outer,POLLIN},1,100); int rv=pf.revents. VERIFIED single-shot: n==1, rv&POLLIN set (nesting works naively). The load-race variant that FAILS loudly: spawn 2 threads each epoll_wait(inner) draining, one thread poll(outer); over N=10000 writes to efd, count outer POLLIN notifications vs inner drains -- a poll-gated drainer (drain inner ONLY when outer reported) leaves undrained events (count mismatch) whereas the runloom fix (drain inner every tick regardless) reaches parity. Diff drained==written.
rules.bugs_addressed: The cov_workload --hubs 4 loop-backend hang (mn_sched_hub_main.c.inc:901-931); the io_uring-loop file_read hang under RUNLOOM_IOURING_LOOP=1; the netpoll pump's unconditional-drain-on-idle invariant that the arm cache correctness rests on ('every hub pumps its OWN epoll when idle AND periodically while busy', PERHUB_EPOLL.md:98-100).

rules.name: arm-cache-is-fd-number-keyed-and-outlives-the-fd
rules.rule: Any per-fd registration/arm cache keyed by fd NUMBER (runloom_fd_armed, runloom_fd_epoll_pool) is aliased across the fd's whole lifetime: closing an fd auto-removes it from the kernel epoll set, but does NOT clear a userspace cache. A reused fd number then inherits a stale 'already armed' entry, the register-once fast path skips the EPOLL_CTL_ADD, and the fd is never (re)registered -> the parked waiter hangs. The cache MUST be cleared on every close path (unregister), and because a socket dropped to CPython's GC closes the raw fd directly (bypassing a monkey-patched close), a self-healing stale-arm probe is needed as a backstop.
rules.source: man epoll(7) NOTES (fd auto-removed from epoll set when all references closed; 'if the fd is reused ... registration will not be affected' -- the kernel forgets, the cache must too); runloom netpoll_register.c.inc:28-52 (arm cache PROCESS-GLOBAL, cleared only by unregister; GC-dropped socket poisons the fd number) and :605-621 (clear the LEVEL arm cache so a reused fd number does not inherit a stale arm); netpoll.c:145-146 (stale-arm probe: process-global cache survives fd close); RUNTIME_GOTCHAS.md epoll section (stale-arm probe self-heal, RUNLOOM_STALE_ARM_PROBE_MS default 250ms, EPOLL_CTL_MOD ENOENT->re-ADD/clear, EBADF/EPERM->clear+error-wake).
rules.footgun: Trusting the kernel's auto-removal-on-close to keep a userspace arm cache in sync. It does not: the kernel drops the registration, the cache still says 'armed', and the next wait_fd on the recycled fd number takes the zero-syscall skip and never re-ADDs. Silent, load-dependent, and specific to fd reuse -- exactly the case unit tests without churn miss (caught by test_netpoll_conformance's fd-reuse case).
rules.probe: Python (3.13t, PYTHONPATH=src, PYGO): open socketpair A, wait_fd(A_read) to arm it, then close A WITHOUT calling netpoll_unregister (simulate GC dealloc: os.close on the raw fd). Open new sockets until one reuses A's fd NUMBER; wait_fd(reused) with a 1s timeout in a goroutine and have the peer make it readable. PREDICTED-IF-CACHE-STALE: the goroutine never wakes (register-once skip fired on the stale arm) -> timeout. WITH the stale-arm probe (default on): it heals within ~250ms and wakes; runloom_stale_arm_heals increments. Diff woke-before-timeout==True and heals>0; fail if it hangs to ceiling. C-level equivalent: ADD fd N LEVEL, close(N), dup2 another socket onto N, epoll_ctl(ep,ADD,N,..) -> EEXIST proves the kernel still has the OLD registration for the number in some races / ENOENT-after-close proves auto-removal -- assert the cache clear path issues MOD/ADD accordingly.
rules.bugs_addressed: The netpoll arm cache stale-arm/fd-reuse hang family (RELIABILITY_PROGRAM.md R7.4 residue); the GC-dropped-socket fd poisoning; the aio sock_* @_release_fd_after -> netpoll_release_if_idle contract (CLAUDE.md aio invariants); the 2026-07-05 fd-poison stale-arm heal landing.

rules.name: eof-is-a-flag-on-the-data-filter-not-a-separate-event
rules.rule: EV_EOF is a bit in the returned kevent's .flags on EVFILT_READ / EVFILT_WRITE, never its own filter or event. A peer close / half-close / RST / refused connect surfaces as the ordinary readiness event with EV_EOF set (and, for a socket error, the errno in .fflags for reads or .fflags/.data depending on filter); there is no EVFILT_EOF and no separate hangup delivery. You must inspect .flags on every readiness event, not wait for a distinct EOF notification.
rules.source: man kevent(2) (FreeBSD 14) EVFILT_READ/EVFILT_WRITE: 'EV_EOF ... set when the reader disconnects ... fflags ... contains the socket error'; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c filt_soread() sets EV_EOF via (so->so_rcv.sb_state & SBS_CANTRCVMORE); libuv src/unix/kqueue.c uv__io_poll (`if (ev->flags & EV_EOF)`); Go src/runtime/netpoll_kqueue.go netpoll (`ev.flags&(_EV_EOF|_EV_ERROR)`)
rules.footgun: Assuming a hangup arrives as its own event (epoll EPOLLHUP mental model) or that a dead socket simply stops delivering. It keeps delivering a readiness event forever with EV_EOF set; code that filters on .filter only and ignores .flags treats the dead fd as normal readiness and its waiter spins on EAGAIN or hangs waiting for bytes that will never come.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); kevent(kq,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); close(sp[1]); struct timespec z={0,0}; int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); assert(n==1); assert(ev.filter==EVFILT_READ); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for anyone expecting a separate EOF filter: */ assert(ev.flags & EV_EOF); printf("EOF-in-flags ok, fflags(errno)=%d\n", (int)ev.fflags);
rules.bugs_addressed: B1 (teardown-tail-hang); runloom netpoll_pump.c.inc:245 reads evs[i].flags & (EV_EOF|EV_ERROR) rather than trusting .filter alone

rules.name: eof-must-be-folded-into-both-directions-because-arms-are-one-shot-single-direction
rules.rule: runloom arms ONLY the requested direction, EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT (netpoll_register.c.inc:501-503). A teardown condition on an fd fires EV_EOF on whichever armed filter(s) the kernel's condition touches -- and in a single kevent scan the EOF may appear on only ONE armed direction even when parkers exist on both. Therefore an EV_EOF/EV_ERROR event must be dispatched to BOTH read and write waiters on that fd, with wake_all semantics, or the direction whose filter did not report is stranded. Do not dispatch teardown strictly by the reporting .filter.
rules.source: man kevent(2) EV_EOF (per-filter, not fd-global); libuv src/unix/kqueue.c uv__io_poll folds EOF into the watcher's full pevents (`revents |= w->pevents & (POLLIN|POLLOUT)` on EV_EOF); Go src/runtime/netpoll_kqueue.go registers BOTH filters persistently (netpollopen: EVFILT_READ and EVFILT_WRITE) precisely so EOF reaches each direction -- runloom, arming one-shot single-direction, must fold instead; runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_pump.c.inc:237-248
rules.footgun: Waking only the direction named by evs[i].filter. A READ parker and a WRITE parker share a dead fd; the kernel returns EV_EOF on EVFILT_READ; a by-direction dispatch wakes the reader, leaves the writer parked forever on a socket that will never become writable-with-progress -> silent teardown-tail hang.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int fd=socket(AF_INET,SOCK_STREAM,0); fcntl(fd,F_SETFL,O_NONBLOCK); struct sockaddr_in a={.sin_family=AF_INET,.sin_port=htons(1),.sin_addr.s_addr=htonl(INADDR_LOOPBACK)}; connect(fd,(void*)&a,sizeof a); /* refused */ int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch[2],ev[2]; EV_SET(&ch[0],fd,EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); EV_SET(&ch[1],fd,EVFILT_WRITE,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); kevent(kq,ch,2,NULL,0,NULL); struct timespec t={1,0}; int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,ev,2,&t); int eof=0; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) if(ev[i].flags&(EV_EOF|EV_ERROR)) eof++; /* PREDICTED-FAIL for by-direction dispatch: eof may be < number of armed directions, proving a non-folding dispatcher strands the unreported direction */ printf("armed=2 eof_events=%d -> fold_required=%d\n", eof, eof<2);
rules.bugs_addressed: B1 fold + B2 wake_all=1 (netpoll_pump.c.inc:248); the 'reader armed in old owner's poller / writer migrated' cross-direction strand

rules.name: ev-oneshot-auto-deletes-the-knote-rearm-every-park
rules.rule: EV_ONESHOT delivers the filter's event exactly once and then deletes the knote from the kqueue (not merely disables it). After a one-shot event is retrieved there is NO registration left; the next readiness transition is not reported until you re-issue EV_ADD. runloom re-arms EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT on EVERY park and keeps the per-fd bitmap for unregister only -- it must never gate the kevent() on 'already armed'.
rules.source: man kevent(2) (FreeBSD) EV_ONESHOT: 'Causes the event to return only the first occurrence of the filter being triggered. After the user retrieves the event ... its knote is deleted'; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c kqueue_scan drops EV_ONESHOT knotes after copyout; runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:455-472,501-503
rules.footgun: A register-once fast path (arm once, then on later parks see the fd's cache bit set and RETURN without re-issuing kevent). The knote is already gone after the first delivery, so the second wait parks on an fd with no kernel registration -> lost wakeup / deadline-only timeout. Same class as the epoll EPOLLONESHOT / EPOLLET register-once trap.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); kevent(kq,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); write(sp[1],"a",1); struct timespec z={0,0}; assert(kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z)==1); read(sp[0],(char[8]){0},8); write(sp[1],"b",1); /* second readiness, NO re-arm */ int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for register-once assumption (expects 1): */ assert(n==0); printf("oneshot auto-deleted, re-arm required: n=%d\n", n);
rules.bugs_addressed: the fd-reuse / register-once-skip lost-wake that the netpoll_register comment (lines 455-467) says test_netpoll_conformance's fd-reuse case caught

rules.name: ev-clear-is-edge-triggered-and-drops-the-add-to-scan-window-edge
rules.rule: EV_CLEAR resets the knote's reported state after retrieval, making it edge-triggered (report state TRANSITIONS, not current level). An edge that occurs after an EV_CLEAR knote's last delivery but is coalesced with no fresh transition is not re-reported. runloom deliberately uses EV_ONESHOT (which re-checks level at each re-ADD) NOT EV_CLEAR for socket readiness, and reserves EV_CLEAR only for the self-pipe wake fd where losing a coalesced edge is harmless (the byte's sole job is to break an idle kevent).
rules.source: man kevent(2) (FreeBSD) EV_CLEAR: 'After the event is retrieved by the user, its state is reset. This is useful for filters which report state transitions instead of the current state'; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c (KN_CLEAR handling clears kn_data/kn_fflags post-copyout); runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:455-462 and self-pipe EV_ADD|EV_CLEAR at netpoll_init.c.inc:86
rules.footgun: Choosing EV_CLEAR for readiness to 'avoid re-arming'. An edge that fired in the gap between the EV_ADD and the next blocking kevent -- or before a parker had finished linking -- is consumed with no waiter present and, being edge-triggered, never refires; the level (unread bytes still in the socket buffer) is invisible. A hung reader with data physically sitting in its recv buffer.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_CLEAR,0,0,NULL); write(sp[1],"x",1); kevent(kq,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); /* edge fired BEFORE this scan; EV_CLEAR delivers it once */ struct timespec z={0,0}; assert(kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z)==1); /* now no NEW edge, but a byte is still unread (level!) */ int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for a level assumption (expects 1 because data remains): */ assert(n==0); printf("EV_CLEAR is edge: unread byte not re-reported n=%d\n", n);
rules.bugs_addressed: the 'old scheme register-once + EV_CLEAR' lost-wake (netpoll_register.c.inc:457-462); confines EV_CLEAR to the self-pipe only

rules.name: ev-add-rechecks-readiness-at-add-time-level-report-on-attach
rules.rule: EV_ADD on a filter whose condition is ALREADY satisfied reports the event on the very next kevent scan -- the attach calls the filter's f_event immediately, so data that arrived before the parker linked (or before the re-ADD) is still delivered. This is why re-arming EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT on every park is race-safe against the 'byte landed between op-EAGAIN and re-park' window: you are not waiting for a future transition, you get the current level once.
rules.source: man kevent(2) EV_ADD 'automatically enables the event unless overridden by EV_DISABLE'; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c kqueue_register -> knote_attach then kn->kn_fop->f_event(kn) evaluated at add (KN_SCAN not required for initial level); libuv src/unix/kqueue.c relies on the same immediate report; runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:468-470
rules.footgun: Believing kqueue only fires on edges after registration (so 'I registered late, I missed the byte'), and adding a compensating manual readiness recheck-then-syscall that double-reads or races. Conversely, assuming EV_ADD is idempotent/no-op when already added -- re-adding a live one-shot direction is exactly how runloom re-checks level.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); write(sp[1],"z",1); /* data arrives FIRST */ int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); /* register AFTER the byte */ kevent(kq,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); struct timespec z={0,0}; int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for edge-only-after-registration assumption (expects 0): */ assert(n==1 && (int)ev.data>=1); printf("EV_ADD level-rechecked pre-existing data: n=%d bytes=%d\n", n, (int)ev.data);
rules.bugs_addressed: underpins the safety of one-shot re-arm-every-park (netpoll_register comment 'kqueue reports a level-ready fd at add time')

rules.name: per-change-errors-come-back-in-the-eventlist-with-ev-error-not-as-kevent-return
rules.rule: When a change in the changelist fails (bad fd, bad filter, ESRCH on delete of a non-existent knote), kevent() does NOT fail the whole call: it returns >= 0 and places a kevent in the eventlist with EV_ERROR set in .flags and the errno in .data. Only certain whole-call errors (EFAULT, EBADF on the kq itself, EINTR, ENOMEM with no room for the error event) make kevent() return -1. To reliably surface per-change errors you must either read the eventlist or force one with EV_RECEIPT. runloom sidesteps this by submitting changes with a zero-length eventlist (kevent(kq,kev,n,NULL,0,NULL)) and treating a non-zero return as failure -- valid ONLY because on a full-error changelist with no eventlist room kevent() returns -1.
rules.source: man kevent(2) (FreeBSD) EV_ERROR: 'If an error occurs ... when processing an element of the changelist and there is enough room in the eventlist, then the event will be placed in the eventlist with EV_ERROR set in flags and the system error in data' and EV_RECEIPT; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c kqueue_register error path (kn_error/EV_ERROR); runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:510 (`kevent(...,NULL,0,NULL)==0` success test)
rules.footgun: Passing a real eventlist buffer alongside changes and then only checking `if (kevent(...) < 0)`. A single bad change is swallowed as an EV_ERROR *event* (return value 0), the code thinks the registration succeeded, and the fd is never actually armed -> lost wakeup with no errno at the call site. Also: relying on kevent(...,NULL,0,...) to report an error is only safe when the eventlist is empty.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,-1,EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_RECEIPT,0,0,NULL); int n=kevent(kq,&ch,1,&ev,1,NULL); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for 'errors come back as return -1': */ assert(n==1); assert(ev.flags & EV_ERROR); assert((int)ev.data==EBADF); printf("per-change error is an EV_ERROR event, retval=%d errno-in-data=%d\n", n, (int)ev.data);
rules.bugs_addressed: validates why runloom's NULL-eventlist submit + return-code check (netpoll_register.c.inc:510) is correct and why a future refactor that adds an eventlist buffer would silently drop registration errors

rules.name: knotes-are-per-kqueue-and-the-kqueue-fd-is-not-fork-inherited
rules.rule: A knote lives in exactly one kqueue: a filter registered on kq A is invisible to kq B, and events for an fd are only delivered on the kqueue it was armed in. So the same hub's kqueue that a parker armed its EV_ADD in must be the one its pump drains. Additionally the kqueue descriptor is NOT inherited across fork(2) -- a forked child's copy of the fd references no valid queue -- so a child must create fresh kqueues (and the fd should be FD_CLOEXEC so execve drops it).
rules.source: man kqueue(2) (FreeBSD): 'The queue is not inherited by a child created with fork(2)'; man kevent(2) knote scoped to its kqueue; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_event.c kqueue_close / filt registration per struct kqueue; libuv src/unix/kqueue.c uv__io_fork re-creates the backend fd; runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:474-479 (per-hub kqueue: register+link+pump agree), netpoll_init.c.inc:70-74 (FD_CLOEXEC) and 525-552 (child re-create)
rules.footgun: Arming a parker in the hub's own kqueue but draining a *different* (default/shared) kqueue in the pump -- the event is delivered to the queue nobody polls, hang. Or in a post-fork child, reusing the inherited kqueue fd: operations behave as on a stale/closed queue (EBADF or silent no-delivery). Sharing one kqueue across hubs reintroduces the kq_lock contention runloom removed.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); int kqA=kqueue(), kqB=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); kevent(kqA,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); write(sp[1],"q",1); struct timespec z={0,0}; /* PREDICTED-FAIL for 'any kqueue sees it': */ assert(kevent(kqB,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z)==0); assert(kevent(kqA,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z)==1); /* fork half: */ if(fork()==0){ int c=kevent(kqA,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); printf("child inherited-kqueue scan=%d (expect 0/EBADF, not parent events)\n", c); _exit(0);} printf("knote is per-kqueue: B=0 A=1\n");
rules.bugs_addressed: the register/pump per-hub-kqueue agreement (netpoll_register.c.inc:474-479); audit finding B8 CLOEXEC leak; fork-child fresh-backend (netpoll_init.c.inc:551)

rules.name: close-auto-removes-the-knote-so-a-reused-fd-number-must-clear-runtime-arm-state
rules.rule: Closing the last descriptor referencing a socket removes all knotes referencing it from every kqueue automatically -- no EV_DELETE is needed (and issuing one after close races fd reuse). But the kernel removal does NOT clear any userspace per-fd 'armed' bitmap the runtime keeps: a raw socket.close() that bypasses the runtime's unregister hook leaves the bit set, and if that fd NUMBER is reused a register that gates on 'already armed' skips the kevent and never arms the new socket. Every close path must clear the runtime's per-fd state; the kevent must not be gated on the stale bit.
rules.source: man kevent(2) (FreeBSD): 'Calling close() on a file descriptor will remove any knotes that reference the descriptor'; FreeBSD sys/kern/kern_descrip.c/knote_fdclose via KQ list; libuv src/unix/kqueue.c uv__platform_invalidate_fd purges pending events for a closed fd; runloom src/runloom_c/netpoll_register.c.inc:463-472 and netpoll_unregister netpoll_register.c.inc:585-605 (no post-close syscall; clears fd bit)
rules.footgun: Trusting a per-fd 'is-armed' cache as an optimization to skip re-registration. A user-owned fd closed with a bare close() (aio bridge sock_* / raw socket.close, no runtime hook) leaves the cache bit set; the kernel already dropped the knote; the reused fd number's register sees the stale bit, returns early, and the new socket's reader parks forever (the fast-socket-churn / test_recvfrom hang). Also: issuing EV_DELETE after close() can hit the wrong (reused) fd.
rules.probe: C (BSD/macOS): int sp[2]; socketpair(AF_UNIX,SOCK_STREAM,0,sp); int kq=kqueue(); struct kevent ch,ev; EV_SET(&ch,sp[0],EVFILT_READ,EV_ADD|EV_ONESHOT,0,0,NULL); kevent(kq,&ch,1,NULL,0,NULL); int old=sp[0]; close(sp[0]); /* knote auto-gone */ int nfd=dup(sp[1]); /* likely reuses old's number */ struct timespec z={0,0}; int n=kevent(kq,NULL,0,&ev,1,&z); /* PREDICTED-FAIL for 'stale arm still valid on reused number': */ assert(n==0); printf("close auto-removed knote; reused-fd(%d==%d?) has NO arm -> runtime must re-register n=%d\n", old, nfd, n);
rules.bugs_addressed: runloom netpoll_release_if_idle / unregister bitmap-clear contract (CLAUDE.md 'loop.sock_* releases the fd's netpoll arm'); the reused-fd register-once-skip lost-wake called out at netpoll_register.c.inc:463-467

rules.name: cq-overflow-flag-is-in-SQ-flags-and-needs-NODROP+GETEVENTS
rules.rule: The CQ-overflow condition is signalled in the SUBMISSION ring's flags word (sq_off.flags & IORING_SQ_CQ_OVERFLOW), NOT the CQ ring flags. On IORING_FEAT_NODROP kernels (>=5.5) overflowed CQEs are retained in a kernel backlog and merged into the visible CQ by ANY io_uring_enter(GETEVENTS); on pre-5.5 kernels without NODROP they are silently DROPPED and cq_off.overflow is incremented. A drainer that only walks the visible CQ ring and never issues a GETEVENTS flush permanently strands every fiber parked on a backlogged completion.
rules.source: /usr/include/linux/io_uring.h:460 (IORING_SQ_CQ_OVERFLOW "CQ ring is overflown"), :511 IORING_FEAT_NODROP, cq_off.overflow field; man io_uring_enter(2) (GETEVENTS/EBUSY). runloom: io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:66-80 flush_cq_overflow reads sq_flags (CORRECT place) and issues GETEVENTS; sets sq_flags at io_uring.c... (sq_off.flags) io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:325.
rules.footgun: Reading the overflow bit from cq_off.flags (the natural guess) returns 0 forever, so the heal never fires. And runloom never checks p.features&IORING_FEAT_NODROP after setup: on a 5.1-5.4 kernel overflow DROPS CQEs, the GETEVENTS flush recovers nothing, and the parked fiber hangs with runloom_iouring_inflight() stuck >0. Multishot recv is the trigger (many CQEs the submitter isn't synchronously draining).
rules.probe: Validated runnable ctypes probe: io_uring_setup(4,&p) -> sq_entries=4, cq_entries=8, features&NODROP; submit 24 NOPs in bursts of 4 without reaping; observe sq_off.flags&IORING_SQ_CQ_OVERFLOW==True while cq_off.flags&(1<<1)==False (proves flag location); visible CQEs stuck at 8; then io_uring_enter(fd,0,0,IORING_ENTER_GETEVENTS) and reap: assert distinct completions reaped==24. FAILS loudly (len(seen)!=24) if you believed overflow drops, or read the flag from cq_off.flags. Full script: scratchpad/probe_overflow.py (ran: '(CQ-ring flags overflow bit, WRONG place) False / CQ_OVERFLOW flag True / total distinct completions reaped 24 expected 24 / PASS').
rules.bugs_addressed: CQ-overflow-strands-parked-fiber invariant (runloom-native, io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:48-62); multishot recv burst overflow that permanently unbalances inflight.

rules.name: registered-eventfd-is-a-decoupled-maybe-work-edge
rules.rule: A ring-registered eventfd (IORING_REGISTER_EVENTFD) is incremented by the kernel once per CQE post, asynchronously and independently of who reaps. Its counter is NOT a reliable count of pending CQEs: an eventfd wake can arrive with an already-empty CQ (an inline reaper won the race), and it fires even for CQEs completed inline inside your own io_uring_enter. Treat it purely as a level/edge 'maybe work' signal: always drain the CQ to empty with a post-clear re-check, and tolerate spurious wakes. Under IORING_SETUP_DEFER_TASKRUN the eventfd is NOT posted at all until io_uring_enter(GETEVENTS) runs.
rules.source: man io_uring_register(2) IORING_REGISTER_EVENTFD / IORING_REGISTER_EVENTFD_ASYNC; liburing io_uring_register_eventfd(3); kernel io_uring/eventfd.c io_eventfd_signal. runloom: io_uring.c:295-309 (EFD_NONBLOCK|EFD_CLOEXEC + register), io_uring_l_buf.c.inc:149-153 drains eventfd in a loop then walks CQ with the draining-flag re-check at :243-251; DEFER_TASKRUN incompatibility documented io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:248-259.
rules.footgun: Registering EPOLL level-triggered without draining the eventfd wedges the pump in a busy spin; assuming 1 eventfd increment == 1 pending CQE double-counts inflight and exits the drain loop early; and enabling DEFER_TASKRUN on the pump-on-eventfd global ring makes the pump sleep forever on epoll(eventfd) because the CQE (and thus the eventfd write) never happens without a GETEVENTS enter -- exactly why runloom refuses DEFER_TASKRUN on the global ring.
rules.probe: Runnable ctypes: setup ring, eventfd(0,EFD_NONBLOCK), io_uring_register(fd,IORING_REGISTER_EVENTFD,&efd,1); submit one NOP with io_uring_enter(fd,1,0,0); reap the single CQE INLINE (advance cq_head) BEFORE reading efd; then read(efd,8) -> observe it returns a counter value >=1 with the CQ now empty. Assert: (bytes_read==8 and counter>=1) while (cq_head==cq_tail). If you modelled eventfd-count==pending-CQEs you would predict counter==0 here; the nonzero read FAILS that model, proving the eventfd is a decoupled notification you must not gate CQ processing on.
rules.bugs_addressed: pump-on-eventfd lost/duplicate wake handling; DEFER_TASKRUN-incompatible-with-eventfd deferral (MEMORY io_uring_multishot_deferred step 5).

rules.name: sqe-off-minus-1-means-current-file-position-gated-by-RW_CUR_POS
rules.rule: For IORING_OP_READ/WRITE/READV/WRITEV, sqe.off == (__u64)-1 means 'use and advance the file's current position' (read(2)/write(2) semantics), gated by IORING_FEAT_RW_CUR_POS (>=5.6). off==0 is a genuine absolute offset 0, never 'current position'. Non-seekable fds (pipe/socket/tty) require off==0 or off==-1; any other value returns -ESPIPE. On a kernel lacking RW_CUR_POS, off==(__u64)-1 is taken literally as offset 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF.
rules.source: /usr/include/linux/io_uring.h:514 IORING_FEAT_RW_CUR_POS; man io_uring_prep_read(3)/io_uring_prep_write(3); kernel commit ba04291eb66c (v5.6). runloom: io_uring_l_do.c.inc:304 and :317 set sqe.off=(uint64_t)offset verbatim from a signed int64 runloom_iouring_off_t (io_uring.h:33).
rules.footgun: runloom's pread/pwrite forward the caller's signed offset unchanged and never check p.features&IORING_FEAT_RW_CUR_POS. A caller passing offset=-1 to mean 'current position' gets 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on a pre-5.6 kernel -> EINVAL or a wild sparse write; a caller passing 0 expecting 'wherever the fd is' silently rewinds to byte 0 and re-reads the same bytes. There is no runtime guard, so the failure mode is kernel-version-dependent silent data corruption.
rules.probe: Runnable: io_uring_setup(4,&p); assert p.features&(1<<3) (RW_CUR_POS) present -> on this host it is (verified). Then, on a temp file containing b'ABCDEF': submit IORING_OP_READ len=3 off=(2**64-1) twice, reap between -> expect b'ABC' then b'DEF' (position advanced). Repeat with off=0 twice -> expect b'ABC' then b'ABC' (no advance). assert results differ. If you believed off=0 meant 'current position' you'd predict the two runs equal; the divergence FAILS that. On a pipe, off=5 -> res==-ESPIPE while off=(2**64-1) -> res>0.
rules.bugs_addressed: file_read/pread offset semantics for the io_uring cooperative-file path (io_uring.c:1-12 rationale).

rules.name: failed-io_uring_enter-consumes-zero-SQEs-roll-sq_tail-back
rules.rule: io_uring_enter submits SQEs from [kernel sqe_head, sq_tail) up to to_submit. If it returns an error -- EAGAIN/EBUSY under resource or CQ-overflow pressure, or EINTR from a pending signal on a submit-only call -- it consumes ZERO SQEs (the kernel's sqe_head does not move). The application MUST roll sq_tail back to its pre-write value on a non-retried failure; otherwise the un-consumed SQE (whose user_data points at a now-dead per-op record) lingers between head and tail, and the very next submit makes the kernel execute that stale SQE first -- permanently shifting every later completion by one op and dereferencing a freed stack frame.
rules.source: man io_uring_enter(2) (to_submit, EAGAIN/EBUSY/EINTR); IORING_FEAT_SUBMIT_STABLE semantics. runloom: io_uring_l_ring.c.inc:199-208 per-hub ring rolls sq_tail back on failure (CORRECT); io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:25-46 submit_eintr retries EINTR only. LATENT BUG: the process-GLOBAL submit io_uring_l_buf.c.inc:128-135 advances sq_tail then on n<0 returns -1 with NO rollback.
rules.footgun: runloom fixed rollback for per-hub SINGLE_ISSUER rings but the global-ring submit_sqe (shared by pread/pwrite/recv/send/multishot-arm/cancel) never rolls back: one EAGAIN/EBUSY enter leaves sq_tail advanced, so the next global submit runs a stale SQE carrying a freed stack op record -> use-after-free + every subsequent completion off-by-one. EINTR is different: it must NOT roll back if you retry, because the retried enter re-submits the same still-queued SQE exactly once (submit_eintr relies on this).
rules.probe: Validated: io_uring_setup(4,&p); write one NOP SQE, set sq_tail=1, call io_uring_enter(fd,1,0,0) UNDER fault injection: `strace -e trace=io_uring_enter -e inject=io_uring_enter:error=EAGAIN:when=1 python3 probe_rollback.py`. Observe enter ret=-1 errno=11 and sq_off.head==0. assert (ret<0 and sq_head==0): kernel took nothing. If you assumed a failed enter still consumes the SQE you'd predict head==1; head==0 FAILS that and proves rollback is mandatory. Ran: baseline 'ret=1 head=1'; injected 'ret=-1 errno=11 head=0 PASS'. Script: scratchpad/probe_rollback.py (mirrors runloom's own strace inject at io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:31).
rules.bugs_addressed: stale-SQE-shifts-all-completions (io_uring_l_ring.c.inc:200-206 fix, missing on the global path); EINTR-corrupts-file_read-into-spurious-OSError (submit_eintr, io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:29-38).

rules.name: ASYNC_CANCEL_FD-is-runtime-5.19-and-the-macro-is-TU-scoped
rules.rule: IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD (cancel in-flight ops keyed by fd instead of user_data, set in sqe.cancel_flags) is a RUNTIME kernel capability added in 5.19; on older kernels the IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL completes -EINVAL and cancels nothing. The compile-time guard `#if defined(IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD)` only reflects whether the BUILD HOST's UAPI header defined the macro -- it says nothing about the running kernel, and in any translation unit that does not #include <linux/io_uring.h> the macro is UNDEFINED, so the guarded block silently compiles to the #else no-op stub even on a fully capable kernel.
rules.source: /usr/include/linux/io_uring.h:325 IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD (1U<<1), :317 comment, cancel_flags sqe field; man io_uring_prep_cancel(3) (fd-keyed cancel, 5.19). runloom: io_uring_l_do.c.inc:98-116 cancel_fd guarded by `#if defined(IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD)`, uses IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD|IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_ALL; io_uring.h:60-76 rationale (io_uring pins the struct file so close() alone won't complete a parked recv). UAPI-macro-TU trap: MEMORY project_pygo_uapi_macro_tu_trap; verify hook commit 70210e51.
rules.footgun: TCPConn.close depends on cancel-by-fd to wake a parked single-shot recv (close(fd) can't, io_uring holds a file ref). Build on 5.19+ headers, run on 5.15 -> the cancel op returns -EINVAL, the recv is never woken, close() hangs until peer traffic. Separately, a cancel helper compiled in the mn_sched TU (which lacks <linux/io_uring.h>) sees IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD undefined and takes the no-op stub, so cancel-by-fd silently does nothing on ALL kernels.
rules.probe:
Two runnable checks. (1) Runtime capability: submit IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL with cancel_flags=IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD|ALL for an fd with no in-flight op; reap CQE.res -> a capable (>=5.19) kernel returns -ENOENT (flag understood, nothing matched); an incapable kernel returns -EINVAL. assert res==-ENOENT. (2) TU trap (verified): `printf '#if defined(IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD)
HAVE
#else
STUB_NOOP
#endif
' | cc -xc -E -P -` prints STUB_NOOP (macro absent without the include) vs prepending '#include <linux/io_uring.h>' prints HAVE. If you assumed the #if tracks kernel support, the STUB_NOOP output FAILS that.
rules.bugs_addressed: close()-doesnt-cancel-parked-recv (io_uring.h:60-67, R7 item 1 hub-ring cancel project_pygo_r7_hubring_cancel); UAPI-macro-TU-trap silent #else stub (project_pygo_uapi_macro_tu_trap).

rules.name: pbuf-ring-tail-is-overlaid-on-buf0.resv-not-a-separate-word
rules.rule: In an IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING provided-buffer ring, the producer tail is a __u16 OVERLAID on the resv field of buffer entry 0 -- byte offset 14 of the 16-byte io_uring_buf (addr:8, len:4, bid:2, resv:2) -- not a standalone counter. ring_entries must be a power of two; the mask is (entries-1). To publish N buffers you fill descriptors [0..N) then store-RELEASE tail=old_tail+N into bufs[0].resv (so the kernel sees all descriptors before the advanced tail). The kernel advances its own head as it consumes; the app only ever advances tail. When recycling a buffer into slot idx==0 you rewrite addr/len/bid but must leave the tail (bufs[0].resv) authoritative.
rules.source: /usr/include/linux/io_uring.h:660-676 struct io_uring_buf_ring (union: {resv1 u64, resv2 u32, resv3 u16, tail u16} overlays bufs[]) + :651-658 struct io_uring_buf; liburing io_uring_buf_ring_add(3)/io_uring_buf_ring_advance(3). runloom: io_uring_l_sys.c.inc:371-392 init (store-release N into bufs[0].resv), io_uring_l_buf.c.inc:66-93 pbuf_return (idx=tail&mask, store-release tail+1), bring_mask=COUNT-1 with pow2 COUNT (io_uring.c:111).
rules.footgun: Treating tail as a separate field, or zeroing bufs[0].resv in a per-slot init loop AFTER publishing, wipes the tail -> the kernel sees zero buffers and every multishot/BUFFER_SELECT recv completes -ENOBUFS. Non-power-of-two ring_entries breaks the mask wrap and desyncs producer/consumer. Omitting the store-release lets the kernel read a descriptor's addr before it is visible -> reads into a garbage buffer. A plain (non-atomic) tail bump races the kernel's acquire-load of tail.
rules.probe: Runnable struct-layout assertion (catches the overlay misread without a kernel): in C, `_Static_assert(offsetof(struct io_uring_buf, resv)==14 && sizeof(struct io_uring_buf)==16, "tail overlays buf0.resv@14");` and `_Static_assert(offsetof(struct io_uring_buf_ring, tail)==14,"buf_ring.tail@14");` -- both compile only if tail truly aliases buf[0].resv. End-to-end: register a 4-entry pbuf ring (IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING), publish 4 by storing tail=4 into bufs[0].resv, arm one IORING_OP_RECV|IOSQE_BUFFER_SELECT buf_group=0 on a socketpair, send 1 byte, reap -> assert res==1 and (flags&IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER) and bid==(flags>>16)<4, NOT -ENOBUFS. Predicting a separate tail word (writing offset 16) yields -ENOBUFS, which FAILS the assert.
rules.bugs_addressed: provided-buffer-ring -ENOBUFS storms / pool accounting (io_uring.c:100-113 sizing rationale, io_uring_l_buf.c.inc pbuf_return).

rules.name: multishot-stays-armed-only-while-F_MORE-set-rearm-on-terminal-CQE
rules.rule: A multishot op (IORING_RECV_MULTISHOT, set in sqe->IOPRIO not rw_flags) submits once and posts one CQE per event while each CQE carries IORING_CQE_F_MORE. The FIRST CQE WITHOUT F_MORE is terminal: the op is disarmed and the app must resubmit to continue. The kernel auto-terminates a multishot recv on -ENOBUFS (buffer ring empty), on socket error, and on EOF (res==0) -- all delivered as a !F_MORE CQE. The buffer id is valid ONLY when IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER is set, read as cqe->flags>>IORING_CQE_BUFFER_SHIFT (16).
rules.source: /usr/include/linux/io_uring.h:339-355 (IORING_RECV_MULTISHOT '...Sets IORING_CQE_F_MORE if more completions expected'), IORING_CQE_F_MORE/F_BUFFER, IORING_CQE_BUFFER_SHIFT; man io_uring_prep_recv_multishot(3). runloom: io_uring_l_do.c.inc:409-423 ms_submit (sqe.ioprio=IORING_RECV_MULTISHOT, flags=IOSQE_BUFFER_SELECT), :453-494 ms_on_cqe (if(!more)h->armed=0; -ENOBUFS/-ECANCELED non-sticky), :629-635 ms_recv re-arms when !armed; bid read at :472 gated on F_BUFFER at :456.
rules.footgun: Assuming a multishot recv stays armed forever: after an -ENOBUFS-driven terminal CQE the kernel delivers nothing more and the reader hangs -- which is why runloom sizes the pbuf pool to N-conns (io_uring.c:104-110) AND re-arms in ms_recv. Reading the bid unconditionally (no F_BUFFER check) yields a garbage buffer id on a terminal/no-data CQE. Putting MULTISHOT in sqe.rw_flags/addr instead of ioprio silently arms a plain single-shot recv that stops after one CQE.
rules.probe: Runnable: socketpair; register a 1-entry pbuf ring; arm IORING_OP_RECV|MULTISHOT|BUFFER_SELECT (ioprio=IORING_RECV_MULTISHOT) buf_group=0. write() two 1-byte messages to the peer WITHOUT returning the first buffer to the ring. Reap CQEs: expect CQE#1 res=1,flags&F_MORE set,flags&F_BUFFER set; then CQE#2 with F_MORE CLEARED and res=-ENOBUFS (pool empty -> auto-disarm). assert (cqe2.flags & IORING_CQE_F_MORE)==0. If you believed multishot re-arms itself across -ENOBUFS you'd predict F_MORE still set / more CQEs coming; the cleared F_MORE FAILS that and proves an explicit resubmit is required.
rules.bugs_addressed: multishot lifecycle FSM ARMED->UNARMED re-arm (io_uring.c:177-249 runloom_ms_table); -ENOBUFS mid-stream stall (io_uring.c:104-110); multishot recv out-of-order stream corruption tie-in (single-drainer, io_uring_l_buf.c.inc:156-172).

api: CPython asyncio event loop + coroutine driver protocol (Task.__step_run_and_handle_result / Future done-callback scheduling / BaseEventLoop call_soon queue), free-threaded CPython 3.13t. Oracle = stock Lib/asyncio at /home/x/.pyenv/versions/3.14.4t/lib/python3.13t/asyncio. All 8 probes below were executed against that stock interpreter and their asserted traces observed.

rules.name: coro-resume-is-send-None-never-send-value
rules.rule: The Task driver resumes a suspended coroutine ONLY via coro.send(None) on a normal wakeup, or coro.throw(exc) on cancel/error injection. It never does coro.send(value). The value of an `await fut` expression is produced INSIDE Future.__await__ by `return self.result()`, not by whatever the driver sends. On a future-completion wakeup the driver's __wakeup path calls self.__step() with no argument on purpose (so the eval loop uses __next__/send(None)), because Future.__await__/__iter__ discard the sent value.
rules.source: CPython 3.13t Lib/asyncio/tasks.py:304 `result = coro.send(None)`, :306 `result = coro.throw(exc)`, :373-388 __wakeup calls `self.__step()` with the explicit comment 'Don\'t pass the value ... Python eval loop would use .send(value) ... which is slower'; Lib/asyncio/futures.py:283-289 Future.__await__ = `yield self` then `return self.result()`.
rules.footgun: Assuming you must feed the resolved value back into the coroutine (coro.send(result)). It LOOKS correct because Future.__await__ throws the sent value away, so every Future-based test passes; but any non-Future awaitable doing `x = yield f` then reading x observes the leaked result instead of None, and the misread also normalizes cross-thread/re-entrant resume of one coro (repro_coro_resume_serialized: serialized resume of a Coro from 4 threads is still UB).
rules.probe:
import asyncio
async def main():
    loop=asyncio.get_event_loop(); captured=[]; fut=loop.create_future()
    class Aw:
        def __await__(self):
            fut._asyncio_future_blocking=True
            sent=(yield fut); captured.append(sent)
    async def waiter(): await Aw()
    t=loop.create_task(waiter())
    loop.call_soon(lambda: fut.set_result(12345))
    await t
    assert captured==[None], ('driver sent %r into coro; stock sends None'%captured)
    print('OK send(None)')
asyncio.run(main())
rules.bugs_addressed: 117 (send-protocol fold: driver must resume with send(None)/throw, repro_coro_resume_serialized/race)

rules.name: call_soon-strict-fifo-but-timers-are-not
rules.rule: call_soon is a strict FIFO queue backed by collections.deque: append at the tail, popleft at the head, each callback run exactly once. _run_once snapshots `ntodo = len(self._ready)` at the top of the ready-drain, so callbacks scheduled BY callbacks during this iteration are deferred to the NEXT iteration (I/O and timers cannot be starved). SEPARATELY: call_at/call_later ties are NOT FIFO in 3.13 — TimerHandle ordering compares self._when only (no insertion-counter tiebreak), so equal-deadline timers fire in heap order, not registration order.
rules.source: base_events.py:823-856 (call_soon docstring 'FIFO ... called exactly once'; _call_soon does `self._ready.append(handle)`), :2044-2062 (_run_once: `ntodo=len(self._ready)`; `for i in range(ntodo): handle=self._ready.popleft()`; comment 'not any callbacks scheduled by callbacks run this time around'); events.py:129-131 TimerHandle.__lt__ returns `self._when < other._when` only.
rules.footgun: (a) Porting call_soon onto a LIFO stack or a dedup set reorders/drops callbacks. (b) Draining self._ready to empty in one pass (while ready: pop) instead of snapshotting ntodo lets a self-rescheduling callback starve the poller / livelock. (c) Believing the old-CPython folklore that equal-`when` call_at fires FIFO and adding an insertion-counter tiebreak to the timer heap — stock 3.13 has none, so your order diverges from the oracle.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
# (1) call_soon strict FIFO + (2) same-iter reschedule deferral
loop=asyncio.new_event_loop(); order=[]
def a(): order.append('a'); loop.call_soon(lambda: order.append('child'))
for cb,tag in [(a,'a'),(lambda:order.append('b'),'b'),(lambda:order.append('c'),'c')]: loop.call_soon(cb)
loop.call_soon(loop.stop); loop.run_forever()
assert order==['a','b','c'], order   # child deferred past stop
# (3) equal-when call_at ties are NOT FIFO
loop2=asyncio.new_event_loop()
async def m():
    w=loop2.time()+0.05; o=[]
    for i in range(30): loop2.call_at(w,o.append,i)
    await asyncio.sleep(0.2); return o
o=loop2.run_until_complete(m())
assert o!=list(range(30)), 'stock 3.13 does NOT FIFO-order equal-when timers'
print('OK fifo/timer')
rules.bugs_addressed: 118 (call_soon/timer FIFO fold, r04_timer_fifo — its 'counter tiebreak' premise is a misread of pre-3.8 CPython)

rules.name: done-callback-registration-order-via-call_soon
rules.rule: set_result/set_exception first flip _state to FINISHED, then call __schedule_callbacks, which SNAPSHOTS the callback list, CLEARS it, and schedules each entry through loop.call_soon in REGISTRATION order. Therefore done-callbacks fire in the order they were added, each exactly once, and each on a later loop iteration — never synchronously inside set_result.
rules.source: futures.py:158-170 __schedule_callbacks: `callbacks=self._callbacks[:]; if not callbacks: return; self._callbacks[:]=[]; for callback,ctx in callbacks: self._loop.call_soon(callback,self,context=ctx)`; :248-258 set_result sets _state=_FINISHED then calls __schedule_callbacks.
rules.footgun: Firing callbacks synchronously inside set_result (re-entrancy: a callback that completes another future mutates loop state mid-set), firing in reverse/undefined order, or not clearing the snapshot first so a callback that re-registers itself loops. Also failing to snapshot-then-clear atomically lets a concurrent add_done_callback double-fire on 3.13t.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
async def main():
    loop=asyncio.get_event_loop(); fut=loop.create_future(); fired=[]
    for i in range(4): fut.add_done_callback(lambda f,i=i: fired.append(i))
    fut.set_result(1)
    assert fired==[], 'callbacks fired SYNCHRONOUSLY inside set_result'
    await asyncio.sleep(0); await asyncio.sleep(0)
    assert fired==[0,1,2,3], ('done-callbacks out of registration order: %r'%fired)
    print('OK cb order')
asyncio.run(main())
rules.bugs_addressed: 119 (done-callback ordering, r19_done_future_pending_cbs), 99 (exactly-once wake accounting — list snapshot+clear prevents lost/double callback)

rules.name: add_done_callback-already-done-uses-call_soon
rules.rule: add_done_callback on a PENDING future appends (fn, ctx) to _callbacks (fired later by __schedule_callbacks). On an ALREADY-DONE future it schedules fn immediately via loop.call_soon — it does NOT run fn synchronously. So a callback added before completion and one added after both dispatch through call_soon, preserving call_soon FIFO (the pre-completion one was enqueued first, runs first).
rules.source: futures.py:217-229 add_done_callback: `if self._state != _PENDING: self._loop.call_soon(fn,self,context=context) else: ... self._callbacks.append((fn,context))`.
rules.footgun: Running the already-done callback synchronously inside add_done_callback (stock defers via call_soon) — changes observable order and re-enters the loop from user code. The mirror bug: appending to _callbacks on an already-FINISHED future, where __schedule_callbacks already ran and will never run again, so the callback is silently lost.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
async def main():
    loop=asyncio.get_event_loop(); fut=loop.create_future(); seq=[]
    fut.add_done_callback(lambda f: seq.append('pre'))   # pending path
    fut.set_result(1)
    fut.add_done_callback(lambda f: seq.append('post'))  # already-done path
    assert seq==[], 'add_done_callback ran a callback synchronously'
    await asyncio.sleep(0); await asyncio.sleep(0)
    assert seq==['pre','post'], ('cross-path ordering wrong: %r'%seq)
    print('OK add_done_callback')
asyncio.run(main())
rules.bugs_addressed: 119 (done-callback ordering), 118 (already-done path funnels through call_soon FIFO)

rules.name: call_soon_threadsafe-wakes-loop-runs-on-loop-thread
rules.rule: call_soon_threadsafe == _call_soon (append handle to the SAME _ready deque, FIFO with same-thread call_soon) + _write_to_self() to unblock a poll-blocked loop. The scheduled callback executes on the LOOP thread, not the caller thread. Completing a Future directly from a foreign thread via fut.set_result is NOT supported: it touches _ready/_callbacks off-thread with no self-pipe wakeup. The sanctioned cross-thread primitive is loop.call_soon_threadsafe(fut.set_result, v) (exactly what run_coroutine_threadsafe / _chain_future use).
rules.source: base_events.py:876-885 call_soon_threadsafe = `handle=self._call_soon(...); ...; self._write_to_self(); return handle`; :541 _write_to_self (self-pipe/eventfd byte); futures.py:389-400 _chain_future uses `dest_loop.call_soon_threadsafe(_set_state, ...)`.
rules.footgun: Implementing call_soon_threadsafe as plain call_soon WITHOUT the self-pipe/eventfd write — the handle sits in _ready but the loop stays parked in epoll/GetQueuedCompletionStatus until some unrelated event arrives (deadlock under load). Under M:N, routing the wakeup/stop to the CALLER thread's scheduler (sched_stop targets the foreign hub) so the real loop thread never stops (r18). Or letting foreign fut.set_result 'work' by luck when the loop happens to be busy.
rules.probe:
import asyncio,threading,time
async def main():
    loop=asyncio.get_event_loop(); fut=loop.create_future()
    def worker():
        time.sleep(0.05); loop.call_soon_threadsafe(fut.set_result,'woke')
    threading.Thread(target=worker).start()
    r=await asyncio.wait_for(fut,2.0)  # hangs->TimeoutError if wakeup missing
    assert r=='woke', r
    print('OK threadsafe')
asyncio.run(main())
rules.bugs_addressed: 100 (cross-thread completion / wrong-scheduler stop, r18_cross_thread_set_result), 118 (threadsafe handle FIFO-shares _ready)

rules.name: buffered-protocol-statemachine-and-single-reader
rules.rule: BufferedProtocol receive path loops: buf=get_buffer(remaining); if len(buf)==0 raise RuntimeError('get_buffer() returned an empty buffer'); copy min(len(buf),remaining) bytes; call buffer_updated(nbytes) with nbytes<=len(buf); repeat until all data delivered — so the SUM of buffer_updated equals len(data) and the state machine is CM [-> GB [-> BU?]]* [-> ER?] -> CL. StreamReader is single-reader: _wait_for_data raises RuntimeError if a second coroutine awaits while _waiter is set; feed_data extends the buffer then wakes the one waiter, and a reader must drain the userspace buffer BEFORE parking on fd-readability.
rules.source: protocols.py:109-158 BufferedProtocol docstring state machine; :200-215 _feed_data_to_buffered_proto (`if not buf_len: raise RuntimeError('get_buffer() returned an empty buffer')`; loops calling buffer_updated); streams.py:493-514 feed_data (`self._buffer.extend(data); self._wakeup_waiter()`), :515-540 _wait_for_data (`if self._waiter is not None: raise RuntimeError(...already waiting for incoming data)`).
rules.footgun: (a) Calling buffer_updated(full_len) when get_buffer returned a SMALLER buffer, or not re-calling get_buffer for the remainder -> silent truncation/overflow. (b) Tolerating a zero-length get_buffer instead of raising. (c) Parking a reader on KERNEL fd-readability while already-buffered bytes sit in the Python/TextIOWrapper buffer (the input()/BufferedReader slurp) -> permanent hang though data is present (repro_input_buffered_hang). (d) Allowing two concurrent readers on one StreamReader.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
from asyncio.protocols import _feed_data_to_buffered_proto
class P:
    def __init__(s,n): s.buf=bytearray(n); s.tr=[]; s.sink=bytearray()
    def get_buffer(s,h): s.tr.append(('GB',h)); return s.buf
    def buffer_updated(s,n): s.tr.append(('BU',n)); s.sink+=s.buf[:n]
p=P(4); _feed_data_to_buffered_proto(p,b'ABCDEFGHIJ')
assert p.tr==[('GB',10),('BU',4),('GB',6),('BU',4),('GB',2),('BU',2)], p.tr
assert bytes(p.sink)==b'ABCDEFGHIJ'
class E:
    def get_buffer(s,h): return bytearray(0)
    def buffer_updated(s,n): pass
try: _feed_data_to_buffered_proto(E(),b'x'); assert False,'no raise on empty buffer'
except RuntimeError as e: assert str(e)=='get_buffer() returned an empty buffer'
async def two():
    r=asyncio.StreamReader()
    async def rd():
        try: await r.readline()
        except RuntimeError as e: return str(e)
    t1=asyncio.ensure_future(rd()); await asyncio.sleep(0)
    t2=asyncio.ensure_future(rd()); await asyncio.sleep(0)
    m=await t2; t1.cancel()
    assert 'already waiting for incoming data' in m, m
asyncio.run(two()); print('OK buffered/reader')
rules.bugs_addressed: 107 (socket/buffering fold, repro_input_buffered_hang: park on fd while userspace buffer non-empty), 99 (single-waiter re-park must stay registered to avoid lost wakeup, repro_simplequeue_lost_wakeup)

rules.name: cancellation-delivery-is-deferred-throw-at-await-point
rules.rule: Task.cancel() does NOT synchronously raise into the coroutine. If parked on a _fut_waiter it calls _fut_waiter.cancel(msg); on success the CancelledError arrives LATER, when that waiter completes-as-cancelled and __wakeup->__step(exc) does coro.throw(CancelledError) at the current await point. With no _fut_waiter it sets _must_cancel=True so the NEXT __step throws. A coro may catch CancelledError but must re-raise to truly cancel; if it returns normally while _must_cancel is set the Task is still marked cancelled. Each cancel() bumps _num_cancels_requested.
rules.source: tasks.py:203-238 Task.cancel (`if self._fut_waiter is not None: if self._fut_waiter.cancel(msg=msg): return True`; else `self._must_cancel=True`; `self._num_cancels_requested+=1`); :306 `result=coro.throw(exc)`; :312-316 `except CancelledError as exc: ... super().cancel()`; :281-296 __step delivers exc.
rules.footgun: Delivering cancellation by throwing into the coro directly from cancel() (stock defers it through the fut_waiter's completion), or by tearing down the fiber/stack without a throw at the await point (skips except/finally cleanup so resources leak), or dropping the request when no _fut_waiter exists (must set _must_cancel so the next resume throws), or not marking a swallow-then-return coroutine as cancelled.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
async def main():
    loop=asyncio.get_event_loop(); log=[]
    async def child():
        try: await asyncio.sleep(100)
        except asyncio.CancelledError: log.append('caught'); raise
    t=loop.create_task(child()); await asyncio.sleep(0.01)
    assert t.cancel() is True
    try: await t
    except asyncio.CancelledError: log.append('propagated')
    assert log==['caught','propagated'], log  # except ran AND propagated, in order
    print('OK cancel')
asyncio.run(main())
rules.bugs_addressed: 107 (timeout/cancel delivery fold), 100 (cross-thread cancel scheduling), 119 (cancel completes the waiter future, driving its done-callbacks)

rules.name: future-exactly-once-completion-and-await-reentrancy-guard
rules.rule: A Future completes exactly once: set_result/set_exception on an already-FINISHED or CANCELLED future raises InvalidStateError — completion is NOT idempotent and must never be double-counted. Future.__await__ yields self exactly once; on resume it asserts `if not self.done(): raise RuntimeError('await wasn\'t used with future')`, so a duplicate/spurious resume before completion is a hard error, and the awaited value is self.result() computed at resume (not accumulated). This exactly-once, no-double-count discipline is the driver-protocol analog of the recursion-count contract a re-entrant/non-blocking acquire must honor.
rules.source: futures.py:248-256 set_result (`if self._state != _PENDING: raise exceptions.InvalidStateError(...)`), :258-280 set_exception (same guard), :283-289 __await__ (`if not self.done(): self._asyncio_future_blocking=True; yield self` then `if not self.done(): raise RuntimeError("await wasn't used with future")`).
rules.footgun: Treating completion as idempotent (second set_result silently ignored or overwrites the first). The sync-primitive twin: a non-blocking re-entrant fast path that returns success WITHOUT incrementing the hold/recursion count, so one later release fully frees the resource while the holder still thinks it holds it — mutual exclusion silently broken (repro_mp_rlock_nonblocking_reentrant: acquire();acquire(False);release() leaves _count()==0). Also: resuming an awaiting coroutine before its future is done and letting it read a stale/None result instead of raising.
rules.probe:
import asyncio
loop=asyncio.new_event_loop(); fut=loop.create_future(); fut.set_result(1)
try: fut.set_result(2); assert False,'second set_result allowed'
except asyncio.InvalidStateError: pass
f2=loop.create_future(); g=f2.__await__()
assert g.send(None) is f2   # first resume yields self, still pending
try: g.send(None); assert False,'reentrant resume not rejected'
except RuntimeError as e: assert str(e)=="await wasn't used with future", e
loop.close(); print('OK exactly-once/reentrancy')
rules.bugs_addressed: 102 (non-blocking re-entrant acquire drops the recursion count, repro_mp_rlock_nonblocking_reentrant), 99 (exactly-once wake accounting)

api: CPython threading + multiprocessing sync primitives, monkey-patched by runloom (M:N stackful-fiber scheduler on free-threaded CPython 3.13t). Ownership/re-entrancy that stock CPython keys on threading.get_ident()/PyThread_get_thread_ident() must be re-keyed to the fiber (runloom_c.current_g()), because many fibers time-share one hub OS thread; wake/park paths must stay foreign-OS-thread-safe (a non-fiber thread must block on the real OS, never park a non-existent g).

rules.name: rlock-owner-is-the-fiber-not-the-os-tid
rules.rule: RLock ownership and recursion depth are per-EXECUTION-IDENTITY: acquire by the current owner bumps _count without touching the inner lock; a non-owner blocks. Stock keys the owner on get_ident(); under runloom that identity MUST be runloom.current()/current_g() (a fiber G) when in a fiber, falling back to the raw OS tid only for genuine foreign threads, because all fibers on one hub share a single get_ident() and would forge each other's ownership.
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py _RLock.acquire (me=get_ident(); if self._owner==me: self._count+=1) L202-209 and release L230-234; runloom src/runloom/monkey/locks.py CoRLock.acquire L94-109 (cur = runloom.current() if _in_fiber() else _real_get_ident()).
rules.footgun: Assuming get_ident() is a stable per-caller identity. Under M:N it is per-HUB-THREAD, so fiber B (same hub as holder A) reads owner==get_ident()==A and takes the reentrant fast path: it enters a section A still holds and the eventual release does a torn RMW / lost increment. This is the mn-identity double-enter.
rules.probe:
import _thread, runloom_c
idents, gs = [], []
def w():
    idents.append(_thread.get_ident()); gs.append(runloom_c.current_g())
    runloom_c.sched_yield_classic()
for _ in range(4): runloom_c.fiber(w)
runloom_c.run()
# Oracle: an execution identity must be UNIQUE per concurrent owner.
assert len(set(idents))==1, 'get_ident collides across fibers -- unusable as owner key'
assert len({id(g) for g in gs})==4, 'fiber G must be the distinct identity'
# Behavioural: with CoRLock, a 2nd fiber must BLOCK, not re-enter.
import runloom.monkey as m
rl=m.CoRLock(); order=[]
def A():
    rl.acquire(); order.append('Aacq')
    for _ in range(5): runloom_c.sched_yield_classic()
    order.append('Arel'); rl.release()
def B():
    runloom_c.sched_yield_classic(); rl.acquire(); order.append('Bacq'); rl.release()
runloom_c.fiber(A); runloom_c.fiber(B); runloom_c.run()
assert order.index('Arel')<order.index('Bacq'), 'B entered while A held -> get_ident-keyed bug'
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 99/100/123: fiber B re-enters fiber A's RLock because they share one hub OS-tid; get_ident()-keyed ownership silently loses mutual exclusion.

rules.name: semlock-recursive-mutex-ismine-is-ostid
rules.rule: A multiprocessing RLock is a SemLock with kind==RECURSIVE_MUTEX(0); its C re-entrancy shortcut ISMINE(o) == (o->count>0 && PyThread_get_thread_ident()==o->last_tid) treats recursion by OS thread id. Under runloom the recursion count and owner MUST be tracked in Python keyed by the fiber, and the C sem_wait/sem_post touched ONLY on the outermost acquire/final release, so the kernel semaphore is the cross-process gate and Python is the per-fiber re-entrancy.
rules.source: CPython Modules/_multiprocessing/semaphore.c ISMINE macro L48, acquire RECURSIVE fast path L319-323, release ISMINE-assert L400-411, last_tid store L384; runloom src/runloom/monkey/executors.py _co_recursive_semlock_methods L302-381 and _patched_semlock_make_methods L384-395 (patches only kind==RECURSIVE_MUTEX).
rules.footgun: Leaving the C SemLock unpatched for RLocks under M:N: because last_tid is a hub OS-tid, a sibling fiber's ISMINE() spuriously passes on the same hub thread and it double-enters (or count underflows on release). Conversely, calling the C acquire while any fiber owns it can hit the _is_mine shortcut on the wrong fiber -- so runloom only calls C sem_trywait when state[0] is None.
rules.probe:
import multiprocessing as mp, _thread
sl = mp.RLock()._semlock
assert sl.kind==0, 'RLock semlock must be RECURSIVE_MUTEX'
assert sl._is_mine() is False and sl._count()==0
sl.acquire()
# Oracle: ISMINE is defined by OS tid == last_tid; here caller IS that tid.
assert sl._is_mine() is True and sl._count()==1
sl.acquire(); assert sl._count()==2   # reentrant bump, no 2nd sem_wait
sl.release(); sl.release()
# The trap: under M:N last_tid would equal EVERY fiber's tid on that hub,
# so _is_mine() cannot distinguish owners -> recursion must live per-fiber.
assert mp.Lock()._semlock.kind==1, 'plain Lock is SEMAPHORE kind, no ISMINE'
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 99/100/123: a RECURSIVE_MUTEX SemLock (Value/Array.get_lock()) lets fiber B re-enter fiber A's hold because ISMINE keys on the shared hub OS-tid.

rules.name: semlock-nonrecursive-has-no-owner
rules.rule: A SemLock with kind==SEMAPHORE(1) (Lock/Semaphore/Event/Condition/Barrier) has NO owner: any thread/fiber may release, and over-release is detected purely by count, not identity -- POSIX release checks sem_getvalue()>=maxvalue (or the HAVE_BROKEN_SEM_GETVALUE trywait dance) and raises ValueError 'released too many times'. So runloom's cooperative patch wraps only ACQUIRE (sem_trywait + _co_sleep backoff); release stays the raw C sem_post with no identity bookkeeping.
rules.source: CPython Modules/_multiprocessing/semaphore.c release non-recursive branch L411-449 (sem_getvalue >= maxvalue -> ValueError), acquire sem_trywait/sem_wait L349-384; runloom src/runloom/monkey/executors.py _co_semlock_acquire L275-299 and the else-branch of _patched_semlock_make_methods L390-394 ('release stays the C sem_post').
rules.footgun: Adding owner/identity tracking (or ISMINE-style checks) to a non-recursive SemLock. There is no owner concept; a cross-fiber/cross-process release is legal by design, and only the count bounds it. Wrapping release cooperatively is both unnecessary and risks turning a legal foreign release into a spurious error.
rules.probe:
import multiprocessing as mp
lk = mp.Lock()   # kind=SEMAPHORE, maxvalue=1, value=1
try:
    lk.release()               # never acquired; already at maxvalue
    raise SystemExit('FAIL: expected ValueError')
except ValueError as e:
    assert 'too many times' in str(e)
# No owner: a DIFFERENT actor than the acquirer may release a counting sem.
s = mp.Semaphore(1); s.acquire()
assert s.get_value()==0
s.release()                       # legal from any context -> no ISMINE
assert s.get_value()==1
rules.bugs_addressed: foreign-thread 93 / mn-identity: guards against over-patching -- non-recursive cross-process locks must remain owner-less; only acquire needs the cooperative backoff.

rules.name: threading-semaphore-counter-invariant
rules.rule: threading.Semaphore holds a counter = initial + #release - #acquire; acquire(blocking=False) returns True and decrements iff value>0, else False without blocking; a blocking acquire waits while value==0; release(n) adds n and wakes n waiters. runloom's CoSemaphore must reproduce this exact counting (value never goes negative; a queued waiter holds NO permit -- value stays 0 while it waits).
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py Semaphore.acquire L472-517 (while self._value==0: ... else: self._value-=1) and release L519-534 (self._value+=n; notify(n)); runloom src/runloom/monkey/events.py CoSemaphore.acquire L269-368 / release L370-390.
rules.footgun: Treating a blocked acquirer as already holding a permit (decrementing value on enqueue). Stock only decrements on the successful return; runloom hands the permit at release() time via w[2] got_permit under the guard. Decrementing early makes value go negative and breaks the release/notify pairing count.
rules.probe:
import runloom.monkey as m
s = m.CoSemaphore(2)
assert s.acquire(blocking=False) is True     # 2->1
assert s.acquire(blocking=False) is True     # 1->0
assert s.acquire(blocking=False) is False    # 0 -> would block, no decrement
s.release(); s.release()                      # back to 2
assert s.acquire(blocking=False) and s.acquire(blocking=False)
assert s.acquire(blocking=False) is False
# Oracle: mirror against stock threading.Semaphore accounting.
import threading
ref = threading.Semaphore(2)
assert [ref.acquire(False), ref.acquire(False), ref.acquire(False)] == [True, True, False]
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 100: counting must survive GIL-off concurrency -- value bookkeeping under the cooperative guard, permit handed at release not at enqueue.

rules.name: bounded-semaphore-bound-is-live-permits-not-waiters
