rules.rule: BoundedSemaphore.release(n) raises ValueError('Semaphore released too many times') iff value+n > initial_value, checked and incremented under ONE continuous hold of the internal lock. The bound is on the live PERMIT count (value) only; queued waiters hold no permit and MUST NOT be counted toward the bound, and a release that hands its permit directly to a waiter never lifts value above the bound.
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py BoundedSemaphore.release L562-575 (with self._cond: if self._value+n > self._initial_value: raise); runloom src/runloom/monkey/events.py CoBoundedSemaphore.release L428-468 (inlines base loop to keep check+increment atomic; explicitly does NOT count len(self._waiters)).
rules.footgun: Counting len(_waiters) in the bound check (spurious ValueError on every legitimate release whenever fibers are queued -- the common case at M:N scale, losing the permit and stranding waiters), OR checking value outside the guard then delegating to base release (a TOCTOU: two hubs releasing at value==initial-1 both pass and both increment, exceeding the bound with no error).
rules.probe:
import runloom.monkey as m
b = m.CoBoundedSemaphore(1)
b.acquire()          # value 1->0
b.release()          # 0->1 (back to bound), OK
try:
    b.release()      # 1+1 > initial(1)
    raise SystemExit('FAIL: expected ValueError')
except ValueError as e:
    assert 'too many times' in str(e)
# Oracle: stock BoundedSemaphore raises identically on over-release.
import threading
rb = threading.BoundedSemaphore(1); rb.acquire(); rb.release()
try:
    rb.release(); raise SystemExit('FAIL')
except ValueError: pass
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 100/123: waiter-count-in-bound regression -- thousands of fibers contending K permits made every release spuriously raise and strand the owed waiters.

rules.name: condition-requires-lock-ownership-and-fifo-waiters
rules.rule: Condition.wait()/notify()/notify_all() require the caller to HOLD the associated lock (stock raises RuntimeError via _is_owned() otherwise); wait() appends a fresh waiter to a FIFO deque, releases the lock across the block, and notify(n) wakes the n leftmost (oldest) waiters. runloom's CoCondition must preserve FIFO wake order and the lock-held precondition, and _is_owned MUST resolve through the fiber-keyed CoLock/CoRLock.
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py Condition.wait L349-372 (if not self._is_owned(): raise), notify L398-426 (waiter=waiters[0]; FIFO), notify_all L428-435; runloom src/runloom/monkey/events.py CoCondition.wait L167-215 (release()/acquire() bracket the park) / notify popleft L231-240.
rules.footgun: Calling wait/notify without holding the lock (stock RuntimeError; runloom surfaces it as CoLock 'release unlocked lock'), or waking in LIFO/arbitrary order. Also: an untimed wait must return ONLY on a real notify -- a spurious pooled-parker wake must RE-PARK, else stock concurrent.futures Future.result(None) re-checks _state and raises a bogus TimeoutError.
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 99 + foreign-thread 90: notify from a real feeder/worker thread and _is_owned() must resolve by fiber identity; FIFO + notify-only-return contract keeps concurrent.futures waiters correct.

rules.name: condition-rlock-recursion-save-restore
rules.rule: When a Condition wraps an RLock, wait() must NOT call the RLock's normal release() (which only decrements one recursion level); it calls _release_save() to fully unlock and capture (count, owner), then _acquire_restore((count, owner)) to restore the exact recursion depth on wake. runloom's CoRLock provides _release_save/_acquire_restore/_is_owned/_recursion_count with parity, and CoCondition.wait snapshots/reinstates _count around the unlocked window.
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py Condition.__init__ binds lock._release_save/_acquire_restore/_is_owned L291-296, wait uses them L355/L367, _RLock._release_save L246-254 / _acquire_restore L242-244 / _recursion_count L261-264; runloom src/runloom/monkey/locks.py CoRLock._release_save L144-151 / _acquire_restore L153-158 / _recursion_count L139-142, events.py CoCondition.wait owned_recursion save/restore L172-214.
rules.footgun: Using RLock.release() inside Condition.wait: a recursively-held RLock (depth>1) would stay locked (only one level dropped), so no notifier's thread/fiber can ever acquire it -> permanent deadlock. Also _recursion_count/_is_owned must key on the fiber, or restore reinstates the wrong owner.
rules.probe:
import threading
c = threading.Condition(threading.RLock())
c.acquire(); c.acquire()              # recursion depth 2
assert c._is_owned() and c._recursion_count() == 2
st = c._release_save()                # must fully unlock
assert c._recursion_count() == 0 and not c._is_owned()
c._acquire_restore(st)                # restore depth 2
assert c._recursion_count() == 2
c.release(); c.release()
assert not c._is_owned()
# runloom parity: same API on CoRLock.
import runloom.monkey as m
rl = m.CoRLock(); rl.acquire(); rl.acquire()
s = rl._release_save(); assert rl._count == 0
rl._acquire_restore(s); assert rl._count == 2; rl.release(); rl.release()
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 99/123: full-unlock-and-restore keyed by fiber so a recursively held Condition lock is actually released across wait() and reinstated to the owning fiber.

rules.name: cooperative-primitive-must-be-foreign-os-thread-safe
rules.rule: A monkey-patched threading primitive taken by a NON-fiber OS thread (e.g. the multiprocessing.Queue _feed daemon, a Thread worker doing self._started.set()) must detect no-goroutine (current_g() is None / TLS peek NULL) and block/wait on the REAL OS (select on a self-pipe fd, os.write to wake), never call runloom_c.park/wait_fd (which parks a goroutine on a hub that does not exist on this thread), and never lazily allocate scheduler state. The wake side to an in-memory fiber parker uses g.wake() (wake_safe), which is the only foreign-safe re-queue.
rules.source: runloom CLAUDE.md 'Cooperative primitives are foreign-OS-thread-safe'; src/runloom/monkey/_base.py _Parker.park foreign branch L372-382 (_raw_select, not wait_fd), CoFMutex class doc + acquire fiber-vs-foreign dispatch L459-571, _unpark_all foreign-setter note L662-674 (os.write is race-free; unpark_many is not from a foreign thread).
rules.footgun: Calling wait_fd/park from a thread with no hub -> undefined, raced, SIGSEGV under M:N; OR a foreign-thread unpark_many that unlinks the parker then re-queues the g non-atomically vs run()'s drain loop, so run() observes netpoll_parked==0 before the ready-push and EXITS, dropping the wake and stranding the fiber (foreign holder then spins try_lock forever).
rules.probe:
import threading, runloom_c, runloom.monkey as m
lk = m.CoLock(); out = []
def worker():
    assert runloom_c.current_g() is None, 'feeder thread must have no fiber'
    lk.acquire(); out.append(1); lk.release()   # must block on real OS, not park a g
t = threading.Thread(target=worker); t.start(); t.join(2)
assert out == [1], 'foreign-thread acquire/release must not park a non-existent g / crash'
# Event.set() from a foreign thread must wake without touching wait_fd.
ev = m.CoEvent()
def setter(): ev.set()
assert ev.wait(0) is False
th = threading.Thread(target=setter); th.start(); th.join(1)
assert ev.is_set() is True
rules.bugs_addressed: foreign-thread 90/92/93: mp.Queue _feed / Thread.start / offload feeder taking a patched Lock/Event/Condition strands a fiber or SIGSEGVs (SIGUSR1-confirmed Once ~1/240, WaitGroup 58/600, CoLock 31/400; p92 offload-parker).

rules.name: rlock-release-nonowner-raises-and-none-owner-guard
rules.rule: RLock.release() by a non-owner raises RuntimeError('cannot release un-acquired lock'); a fresh/released RLock has owner==None. runloom must guard the None case: a caller whose fiber identity is ALSO None (a forked child running threading._after_fork with no live scheduler) must NOT be mistaken for the owner -- the owner==cur test is gated by 'self._owner is not None' so the impostor takes the real inner acquire instead of a phantom reentrant bump.
rules.source: CPython Lib/threading.py _RLock.release L230-234 (if self._owner != get_ident(): raise); runloom src/runloom/monkey/locks.py CoRLock.acquire None-guard L94-104 ('self._owner is not None and self._owner == cur') and release fork-detect L111-124.
rules.footgun: Testing only owner==cur: with a fresh lock (owner None) and a caller identity that is also None (forked child, or any context where current_g() and get_ident fallback both degrade to None), the reentrant fast path fires, skips the real acquire, and then release() blows up with 'release unlocked lock' -- or double-frees the underlying mutex.
rules.probe:
import runloom.monkey as m
rl = m.CoRLock()
assert rl._owner is None and rl._count == 0
try:
    rl.release()          # never acquired
    raise SystemExit('FAIL: expected RuntimeError')
except RuntimeError as e:
    assert 'un-acquired' in str(e) or 'release' in str(e)
# Oracle: stock _RLock matches.
import threading
try:
    threading._PyRLock().release(); raise SystemExit('FAIL')
except RuntimeError: pass
# None-owner guard: a fresh lock's None owner must not swallow a real acquire.
assert rl.acquire() is True and rl._owner is not None; rl.release()
rules.bugs_addressed: mn-identity 99/123: forked-child / no-scheduler identity==None must not alias the fresh-lock owner==None and skip the real acquire (the 'release unlocked lock' fork-teardown crash).

rules.name: semlock-after-fork-rezeroes-net-acquisition-count
rules.rule: SemLock._after_fork() rezeroes count (the net acquisition count) so a child that inherited a held cross-process lock does not carry the parent's recursion depth. runloom mirrors this: CoLock/CoRLock/CoFMutex._at_fork_reinit reset to a fresh unlocked state and null the per-fiber owner/count, because the child inherits none of the parent's fibers -- and cold release paths detect _is_forked_child() to swallow benign post-fork teardown releases rather than raise.
rules.source: CPython Modules/_multiprocessing/semaphore.c _after_fork sets self->count=0 L676-683; runloom src/runloom/monkey/locks.py CoLock._at_fork_reinit L72-77 / CoRLock._at_fork_reinit L160-163, _base.py CoFMutex._at_fork_reinit L614-618, _is_forked_child L36-37 (pid-mismatch) with release() fork-swallow L58-67.
rules.footgun: Carrying the parent's count/owner into the child: after fork() only the forking thread survives, so a captured owner G / recursion depth is meaningless in the child; a child running os.register_at_fork teardown (logging, concurrent.futures) would hit 'release unlocked lock' or an ISMINE assert on a lock whose matching acquire happened in the parent.
rules.probe:
import multiprocessing as mp
sl = mp.RLock()._semlock
sl.acquire(); sl.acquire()
assert sl._count() == 2
sl._after_fork()              # child-side rezero of net acquisition count
assert sl._count() == 0, 'count must rezero after fork'
# runloom parity: _at_fork_reinit resets owner/count to fresh-unlocked.
import runloom.monkey as m
rl = m.CoRLock(); rl.acquire()
rl._at_fork_reinit()
assert rl._owner is None and rl._count == 0 and rl._lock.locked() is False
rules.bugs_addressed: foreign-thread 90/93: post-fork teardown of an inherited lock -- rezero count + fork-detect swallow so stdlib _after_fork handlers don't raise on a lock the parent held.

api: CPU/ABI context-switch contract for stackful fibers (runloom_asm_swap / runloom_asm_make_ctx): the register + FP-env + stack-alignment state a swap MUST save/restore per arch, plus the CPython 3.13/3.14t thread-state recursion/C-stack-guard fields that must be re-armed per fiber. Grounded in src/runloom_c/arch/swap_{x86_64,aarch64}.S, fcontext.c, runloom_iframe.c, module.c; runloom bugs 11/12/13.

rules.name: x86-64 MXCSR control bits are callee-saved -> swap MUST save/restore them
rules.rule: On System V AMD64 the SSE control/status register MXCSR has callee-saved CONTROL bits (rounding mode bits 13-14, exception masks bits 7-12, FTZ bit 15, DAZ bit 6). A stackful swap that changes hubs/fibers must STMXCSR before storing SP and LDMXCSR after loading the new SP, or a fiber's fesetround/feenableexcept leaks into the next fiber/scheduler on the same hub: silent wrong SSE arithmetic, or a later normal op turning into a process-killing SIGFPE. The xmm register *contents* are caller-saved and correctly skipped; only the MXCSR word is preserved.
rules.source: System V AMD64 ABI v1.0 sec 3.2.1 Fig 3.4 register-usage table ('the control bits of the MXCSR register are callee-saved (preserved across calls)'); runloom impl src/runloom_c/arch/swap_x86_64.S:76 (stmxcsr) / :80 (ldmxcsr); fresh-fiber seed fcontext.c:42 (0x1F80).
rules.footgun: Assuming 'xmm is caller-saved so FP needs nothing saved' -- true for the *data* xmm0-15 but false for the MXCSR control word. A swap that skips MXCSR passes fuzz/soak green because generic soak never checks rounding-mode leakage across a switch (why bug 13 was caught by review, not the suite).
rules.probe: Build the probe that drives the REAL swap: a fiber sets MXCSR round-toward-+inf (mov getcsr; setcsr (mx&~0x6000)|0x4000) then swaps back; main records MXCSR before/after. Runnable C (validated): cc -O2 probe.c src/runloom_c/arch/swap_x86_64.S && ./probe -> prints 'mxcsr_preserved=1 (mx0=0x1f80 mx1=0x1f80)'; a swap with the stmxcsr/ldmxcsr lines deleted prints mx1=0x5f80 (leaked) -> FAIL. Python equivalent (validated on 3.13t): fiber calls libc.fesetround(FE_UPWARD=0x800) then runloom_c.yield_(); after c.resume(), (1.0 + 2.0**-53) must == 1.0 (round-to-nearest); ==1.0000000000000002 means the mode leaked.
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 13 (aarch64 FPCR / earlier x86 MXCSR+x87 not saved across fiber switch; data-loss). tests/bughunt_repros/fpenv.py, fpenv2.py, fpenv_mn.py.

rules.name: x86-64 x87 control word (FCW) is callee-saved -> swap MUST save/restore it separately from MXCSR
rules.rule: The x87 FPU control word (rounding-precision PC bits 8-9, rounding-control RC bits 10-11, exception masks bits 0-5) is callee-saved on SysV AMD64, distinct from MXCSR. A swap must FNSTCW/FLDCW it in addition to STMXCSR/LDMXCSR: long-double math, C99 fesetround (which sets BOTH x87 and SSE state), and feenableexcept touch the x87 CW, so omitting it leaks precision/rounding/trap-enable state into the next fiber. It shares the same 16-byte FP-env slot as MXCSR (MXCSR at +0, FCW at +8) so the saved frame stays a multiple of 16.
rules.source: System V AMD64 ABI v1.0 sec 3.2.1 Fig 3.4 ('the x87 status word register is caller-saved, whereas the x87 control word register is callee-saved'); runloom impl swap_x86_64.S:77 (fnstcw 8(%rsp)) / :81 (fldcw); fresh-fiber seed fcontext.c:44 (0x037F, PC=64-bit, all masked).
rules.footgun: Saving MXCSR but not FCW because 'nobody uses x87 anymore' -- glibc fesetround/feenableexcept still program the x87 CW, and long double is x87 on Linux; a half-saved FP env leaks rounding/trap-enable on the x87 side even when SSE looks clean. Also: reserving only 8 bytes for the slot instead of 16 breaks trampoline alignment math (post-ret SP must match the no-FP-env layout).
rules.probe: In the validated GPR/FP C probe, additionally clobber the x87 CW in the fiber (fnstcw/or-in RC=round-up/fldcw) and read it in main via fnstcw before/after; a swap missing the fnstcw/fldcw pair returns the fiber's modified CW to main. Cross-check the 16-byte slot: assert ((uintptr_t)(new_sp)%16)==8 at the trampoline call boundary (see the alignment probe) -- a 24-byte or 8-byte slot flips that parity and MOVAPS in the fiber #GP-faults.
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 13 (x86 x87 control word not saved across fiber switch; data-loss).

rules.name: aarch64 FPCR is callee-saved -> swap MUST save/restore it; FPSR is caller-saved (do NOT waste a swap on it)
rules.rule: On AArch64 the FPCR (rounding mode RMode bits 22-23, Flush-to-zero FZ bit 24, exception-trap enables bits 8-15, AH/alt-handling) is call-preserved: a subroutine that changes it must restore it. A stackful swap must MRS x9,fpcr before storing SP and MSR fpcr,x9 after loading the new SP. FPSR (cumulative exception STATUS flags QC/IDC/etc) is NOT call-preserved -- correctly omitted. The FPCR gets a 16-byte slot (FPCR at +160, +168 padding) so the 160-byte GPR/FP frame stays 16-aligned (176 = 11*16). Fresh fiber seeds FPCR=0 = AArch64 default (round-to-nearest-even, no traps, FZ off).
rules.source: AAPCS64 (Arm IHI 0055, 'Procedure Call Standard for the Arm 64-bit Architecture') sec 6.2.2 SIMD/FP and 6.2.3 (FPCR bits RMode/FZ/exception-enables are call-preserved; FPSR is not); runloom impl swap_aarch64.S:60-61 (mrs/str fpcr) / :81-82 (ldr/msr); fresh-fiber seed fcontext.c:82 (FPCR slot zeroed). Reference: Boost.Context / Go runtime asm save FPCR.
rules.footgun: Symmetry error with x86: assuming 'FPCR is like FPSR, status, caller-saved' and skipping it -- then a fiber that set a rounding mode or enabled FP traps corrupts the next fiber. Inverse waste: saving FPSR too (it isn't preserved, so restoring a stale FPSR wrongly clears/sets cumulative exception flags the caller expected to read).
rules.probe: aarch64 C probe against swap_aarch64.S: fiber does mrs x/orr RMode=0b01(toward +inf)/msr fpcr then swaps back; main mrs fpcr before/after -> must be identical. Deleting the mrs/msr fpcr pair makes main read the fiber's RMode. Python (on an M2/Graviton build): same fesetround(FE_UPWARD) yield_ repro as x86 -- tests/bughunt_repros/fpenv.py -- (1.0+2**-53)==1.0 must hold. SYSTEMATIC_IMPROVEMENTS note that generic soak has NO FPCR oracle, so this dedicated diff is the only detector.
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 13 (aarch64 FPCR not saved across fiber context switch; data-loss on M2; commit 60c2eb2c).

rules.name: x86-64 callee-saved GPR set is exactly {rbx, rbp, r12, r13, r14, r15} -> swap saves those, and make_ctx must seed them
rules.rule: SysV AMD64 callee-saved integer registers are rbx, rbp, r12-r15 (rsp is special). rdi/rsi/rdx/rcx/r8-r11/rax are caller-saved and MUST NOT be relied on across the swap. The swap pushes all six then the FP-env slot; make_ctx must lay down a matching fake frame (r15,r14,r13,r12,rbx,rbp then the return-IP slot) with the coro pointer pre-loaded into the r12 slot (the trampoline reads r12) and the trampoline address in the return slot. Any mismatch between the push order in swap and the frame order in make_ctx corrupts the first swap-in.
rules.source: System V AMD64 ABI v1.0 sec 3.2.1 Fig 3.4 (callee-saved: rbx, rbp, r12-r15); runloom impl swap_x86_64.S:57-62 (push order rbp,rbx,r12,r13,r14,r15) and make_ctx fcontext.c:45-51 (frame[5]=coro into r12 slot, frame[8]=trampoline).
rules.footgun: Off-by-one between swap's push order and make_ctx's frame array (e.g. seeding coro into the r13 slot) -> first resume jumps with a garbage coro pointer. Or trusting a caller-saved reg (r10/r11) to survive the swap because it happened to on one compiler. Note the trampoline's single `subq $8` re-aligns after ret popped 8 -- it depends on the FP-env slot being 16, not 8, bytes.
rules.probe: Validated runnable C: main puts sentinels in r12 (0xCAFE12) and rbx (0xCAFEbb) via register-asm vars, swaps into a fiber that clobbers all of rbx/r12/r13/r14/r15 (mov $0xdead*,%reg) then swaps back; main asserts r12==0xCAFE12 && rbx==0xCAFEbb. Command: cc -O2 probe_gpr.c src/runloom_c/arch/swap_x86_64.S && ./probe_gpr -> 'gpr_preserved=1'. Removing any pushq/popq pair from the swap flips it to 0 -> FAIL loudly.
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 13 lineage (the register-save contract the FP-env fix sits inside); make_ctx frame-layout correctness (fcontext.c:31).

rules.name: aarch64 callee-saved set is x19-x28 + x29(FP) + x30(LR) + d8-d15 low-64; x18 is the reserved PLATFORM register
rules.rule: AAPCS64 callee-saved: general x19-x28, plus x29 (frame pointer) and x30 (link register) which the swap saves as the pair at [sp,#80]; SIMD/FP d8-d15 (only the LOW 64 bits of v8-v15 are preserved -- upper bits are caller-saved). The swap saves 12 GPRs + 8 doubles + FPCR = 176 bytes and RETs to the saved x30. CRITICAL: x18 is the platform register -- reserved on Apple/Windows and by many ABIs -- and MUST NOT be used as scratch or saved/restored by the swap; touching it corrupts TLS/stack-guard state the OS keeps there. make_ctx seeds x19 with the coro pointer (trampoline reads x19) and x30 with the trampoline address.
rules.source: AAPCS64 (Arm IHI 0055) sec 6.1.1 (x19-x28 callee-saved; x18 platform register 'must not be used'; x29 FP, x30 LR) and 6.1.2/6.2.2 (v8-v15 low 64 bits callee-saved, upper caller-saved); runloom impl swap_aarch64.S:50-59 (stp x19..x30, d8..d15) and make_ctx fcontext.c:83-84 (x19=coro, x30=trampoline).
rules.footgun: Saving/using x18 'because it's a spare high register' -> clobbers the platform's per-thread value (SSP/shadow-stack or TLS base on some targets) and produces platform-specific corruption invisible on Linux. Also: saving the full 128-bit v8-v15 (wasteful) or forgetting that ONLY the low 64 bits are preserved (over-trusting the upper lanes across a swap).
rules.probe: aarch64 C probe: main seeds sentinels in x19-x28 and d8-d15 via register-asm vars, swaps into a fiber that overwrites all of them + FPCR, swaps back, main asserts each survived. Separately, a static check on the .S: assert x18 appears in NO stp/ldp/mov target in swap_aarch64.S (grep for 'x18' must be empty) -- a regression that spills x18 is a silent platform-ABI violation.
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 13 (aarch64 register/FP-env save contract, commit 60c2eb2c swap_aarch64.S).

rules.name: Stack MUST be 16-byte aligned at every call boundary (SysV: rsp%16==8 at callee entry; AAPCS64: SP%16==0 at public interface)
rules.rule: SysV AMD64 requires rsp to be 16-byte aligned at the point of a CALL, so the callee's first instruction sees rsp%16==8 (return address pushed). make_ctx must deliberately misalign by 8 (sp &= ~15; sp -= 8) BEFORE laying the saved frame so that after the swap's ret and the trampoline's single `subq $8`, the callq into entry() is 16-aligned; the FP-env slot must be 16 bytes (not 8) to keep this parity. AAPCS64 requires SP 16-aligned at every public interface and forbids SP ever being non-16-aligned when used as a base. Misalignment #GP-faults any MOVAPS/aligned SSE spill and breaks stack-probing.
rules.source: System V AMD64 ABI v1.0 sec 3.2.2 ('the value (%rsp+8) is always a multiple of 16 when control is transferred to the function entry point'); AAPCS64 (IHI 0055) sec 6.2.2 / 6.4.6 (SP 16-byte alignment at public interfaces); runloom impl fcontext.c:35-39 (sp&=~15; sp-=8; ... sp-=16 FP slot) and trampoline swap_x86_64.S:108-109 (subq $8 before callq); aarch64 fcontext.c:78 (sp&=~15).
rules.footgun: Reserving an 8-byte or 24-byte FP-env slot 'to save space' flips the post-ret parity so the trampoline's subq $8 lands entry() on a 16-misaligned stack -> the FIRST aligned SSE spill in any Python/C code the fiber runs SIGSEGVs, intermittently and only with -O2 (where the compiler emits MOVAPS). Assuming alignment is free because 'malloc returns 16-aligned' ignores the return-address parity.
rules.probe: Validated runnable C: a fiber calls aligned_work() which does __attribute__((aligned(16))) float buf[4]; _mm_store_ps(buf, v) (emits MOVAPS). cc -O2 probe_align.c src/runloom_c/arch/swap_x86_64.S && ./probe_align -> 'stack 16-aligned OK'; change the FP slot in make_ctx from 16 to 8 (or drop the trampoline's subq $8) and the same binary SIGSEGVs in aligned_work -> the misalignment is caught deterministically.
rules.bugs_addressed: make_ctx/trampoline alignment correctness (fcontext.c:30-37, swap_x86_64.S:107-109); the invariant the bug-13 FP-env 16-byte slot preserves.

rules.name: x86-64 red zone (128 bytes below rsp) must not be clobbered by asynchronous writes across a switch
rules.rule: SysV AMD64 reserves the 128 bytes below rsp (the red zone) for a leaf function's scratch use without adjusting rsp. A cooperative swap preserves it automatically because it lives in the outgoing fiber's stack region (never freed while the fiber exists). But: (1) a signal-based preemption or any code that runs on the fiber's stack via a signal handler MUST be delivered on an alternate signal stack (sigaltstack) or with the red zone honored, else it corrupts the interrupted leaf frame; (2) the swap itself must not write scratch below the *incoming* SP before it has finished restoring -- all swap state lives at/above the loaded SP. AArch64 has no red zone (SP is a hard low-water mark), so signal frames there must not assume one.
rules.source: System V AMD64 ABI v1.0 sec 3.2.2 ('a 128-byte area beyond the location pointed to by %rsp is considered to be reserved and shall not be modified by signal or interrupt handlers'); Linux honors it via SA_ONSTACK/sigaltstack. AArch64: no red zone (AAPCS64 6.2.2, SP defines the valid stack extent). runloom preemption path: module_run.c.inc / preempt_init and the crash handler stack (runloom_crash.c).
rules.footgun: Installing a preemption/crash SIGSEGV handler WITHOUT SA_ONSTACK/sigaltstack: the kernel builds the signal frame just below the interrupted fiber's rsp, silently overwriting the red-zone scratch of an interrupted leaf function -> corruption that only appears when a signal lands mid-leaf-function, i.e. under load on the non-dev box. Also porting an x86 red-zone assumption to aarch64 where none exists.
rules.probe: C probe: a leaf function stores a sentinel into the red zone (mov $0xREDZONE,-8(%rsp)) WITHOUT decrementing rsp, then raises a signal (raise(SIGUSR1)) whose handler is installed WITHOUT SA_ONSTACK and does deep work; on return, assert -8(%rsp) still == 0xREDZONE. It fails (handler clobbered it) without SA_ONSTACK and passes with it -- proving the runloom preempt/crash handlers must set SA_ONSTACK. Grep runloom_crash.c / preempt setup for sigaltstack + SA_ONSTACK as the static guard.
rules.bugs_addressed: context-switch/preemption stack-safety contract adjacent to bugs 12/13 (fiber stacks + signal delivery INTO the parked goroutine, per CLAUDE.md scheduler invariant).

rules.name: CPython 3.14t SP-based C-stack guard (c_stack_soft_limit/hard_limit) MUST be re-armed to THIS fiber's stack on EVERY resume, with extra reserved margin
rules.rule: 3.14 replaced the integer c_recursion_remaining counter with an SP-vs-limit check: PyUnstable_ThreadState_SetStackProtection(ts, base, size) sets soft_limit=base+2*MARGIN and hard_limit=base+MARGIN (stack grows down; _Py_MakeStackCheck raises RecursionError once SP descends below soft_limit). Because runloom's default mode shares ONE per-hub PyThreadState across many fibers that each have their own mmap stack, the limit is left pointing at whichever fiber entered last -- so a parked-then-resumed deep recurser runs off its own stack into the PROT_NONE guard page -> SIGSEGV. Fix: register a pre-swap hook that re-arms the limits at (base,size) of the fiber being resumed, every resume (also tracks copy-grow). Additionally the arm MUST reserve extra headroom (RESERVE = clamp(size/8, 96KB, size/2)); arm against base+RESERVE so RecursionError trips RESERVE bytes early -- the default 32KB (2*16KB MARGIN) gap is NOT enough because CPython's datastack-chunk path (_PyThreadState_PushFrame->push_chunk->allocate_chunk->mmap) itself burns several KB of C stack BELOW soft_limit and writes into the guard page before the check fires.
rules.source: CPython 3.14: Include/cpython/pystate.h c_stack_top/c_stack_soft_limit/c_stack_hard_limit; Python/pystate.c _Py_InitializeRecursionLimits and PyUnstable_ThreadState_SetStackProtection; Include/internal/pycore_ceval.h _Py_MakeStackCheck + _PyOS_STACK_MARGIN_BYTES (16KB); datastack path Python/pystate.c push_chunk/allocate_chunk. runloom impl: runloom_iframe.c:190 runloom_arm_fiber_stackprot (RESERVE_MIN 96KB, iframe.c:189), pre-swap hook module.c:148 runloom_coro_rearm_stackprot + coro.h:71 runloom_coro_pre_swap.
rules.footgun: Arming the SP limit ONCE at fiber entry (like the 3.13 counter snapshot) instead of every resume -> the next fiber to enter overwrites the shared tstate's limit, and the first fiber's deep recursion faults into its guard page. Second footgun: arming against the RAW (base,size) with only the stock 32KB margin -> the mmap/chunk-alloc burst punches the guard (p212: faults in allocate_chunk on descent, munmap on migrated unwind, SP within 25-55 frames of soft_limit). Counter-intuitively a BIGGER fiber stack makes it WORSE (deeper recursion, more chunk churn) -- proving it's the thin margin, not the size.
rules.probe: On free-threaded 3.14t: PYTHON_GIL=0 spawn 2+ fibers on one hub via runloom_c.mn_fiber/go; fiber A recurses ~N deep to just above its RecursionError trip and parks (yield_) mid-recursion; fiber B enters and recurses on ITS stack; resume A and let it recurse one level deeper. Correct (re-armed per resume): A gets a clean Python RecursionError. Broken (armed once / raw margin): SIGSEGV in allocate_chunk or munmap. In-repo driver: tests/big_100/p212_recursion_budget_per_g.py. Static check: assert runloom_coro_pre_swap != NULL after PyInit on 3.14t (module.c registration).
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 12 (3.14 SP recursion check armed on wrong stack, never re-armed on resume, thin margin; crash; commits 4970b3a6, 67f72d35; p212).

rules.name: CPython 3.13t integer recursion counters (py_recursion_remaining + c_recursion_remaining) MUST be snapshotted per-fiber at yield and restored at resume
rules.rule: On 3.13 (incl. 3.13t) recursion overflow is an integer countdown living on PyThreadState: py_recursion_remaining (Python-frame budget) and c_recursion_remaining (C-call budget, default limit checked by _Py_MakeRecCheck as `c_recursion_remaining-- < 0`). Since one per-hub tstate is shared across fibers, each fiber must save both counters into its own per-coro snapshot at yield and restore them at resume -- otherwise a fiber that recursed deep leaves a near-exhausted counter that spuriously RecursionErrors (or under-counts) the next fiber. This is the 3.13 analogue of the 3.14 SP re-arm: same shared-tstate problem, different mechanism. Do NOT also try to snapshot the raw frame-chain pointer (current_frame) here -- that is privatized on its own path; this rule is only the two integer budgets.
rules.source: CPython 3.13t: Include/cpython/pystate.h:112 py_recursion_remaining, :115 c_recursion_remaining; Include/internal/pycore_ceval.h:192 _Py_MakeRecCheck (`tstate->c_recursion_remaining-- < 0`). runloom impl: module.c:73-131 RunloomTstateSnapshot + runloom_tstate_save/restore (version-gated: 3.14 snapshots only py_recursion_remaining, 3.12/3.13 both, 3.11 recursion_remaining, older recursion_depth).
rules.footgun: Snapshotting only py_recursion_remaining on 3.13 and forgetting c_recursion_remaining (the C-call budget) -> C-heavy fibers (json, pickle, C-recursing extensions) mis-account and either spuriously raise or fail to catch a real C-stack overflow. Or applying the 3.14 SP-rearm code path on 3.13 (where the c_stack_* fields don't exist) / vice-versa -- the disposition is strictly version-gated at 0x030E0000.
rules.probe: On 3.13t: fiber A recurses to near c_recursion_remaining exhaustion then yield_(); fiber B (fresh) must be able to recurse to the full default depth -- if A's depleted counter leaked, B raises RecursionError far too early. Assert B's max achievable depth == a control single-fiber run. Instrument via runloom_c introspection or a ctypes read of tstate. Version guard check: the snapshot struct must compile to py_recursion_remaining+c_recursion_remaining on 3.13 and py_recursion_remaining-only on 3.14 (module.c:73-83 #if ladder).
rules.bugs_addressed: bug 12 lineage / the 3.13 half of the recursion-limit-per-fiber contract (module.c snapshot); complements the 3.14 SP re-arm.
